13.2: "Set apart for me every firstborn — every womb's first to open, human or animal, among my people. They belong to me."
13.3: Moses addressed the people: "Burn this day into memory — the day you walked out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. Yahweh brought you out by sheer force of power. No leaven shall be eaten — let nothing rise that day."
13.4: Today — in the month of Abib, the month of ripening grain — you march out free.
13.5: When Yahweh brings you into the land he swore to your fathers — Canaan, a country rich as flowing milk and honey — you shall keep this annual rite. The land and the memory of liberation belong together.
13.6: Seven days of unleavened bread — no leaven in the house, no leaven in the memory — and on the seventh day a festival before Yahweh.
13.7: Unleavened bread only, for the full seven days. No leaven anywhere in your homes or your land — Egypt's bread must not follow you into freedom.
13.8: Tell your children on that day: 'This feast is because of what Yahweh did for me — personally, for me — when he brought me out of Egypt.' Make the exodus your own story, not only your ancestors'.
13.9: Let this rite be like writing on your hand and a mark between your eyes — something you cannot ignore — so that Yahweh's teaching stays on your lips. A strong hand brought you out; let that strength shape your thinking.
13.10: Observe this rite at its proper time, year after year, without fail.
13.11: When Yahweh fulfils his sworn oath and delivers Canaan into your hands,
13.12: set apart for Yahweh every firstborn — the womb's first claim belongs to the one who redeemed you. Every firstborn male of your animals is his.
13.13: The firstborn donkey — redeem it with a lamb, or break its neck; it cannot be left unclaimed. Every firstborn son must also be redeemed — their lives belong to the God who spared them.
13.14: When your children one day ask, 'Why do we do this?' — tell them the story: 'Yahweh brought us out of Egypt by sheer force, out of the slave house.' Give them the narrative before you give them the rules.
13.15: Pharaoh hardened himself and refused to release us — so Yahweh struck every firstborn in Egypt, human and animal alike. The cost of Pharaoh's defiance was the death of Egypt's future. That is why every firstborn male we dedicate to God, and every firstborn son we redeem — because our sons survived only by his mercy.
13.16: Wear it like a sign on your hand, like scripture between your eyes — because Yahweh's strong hand brought us out. Let the body remember what the mind might forget.
13.17: When Pharaoh released them, God did not take them the short road through Philistine territory. The reason was pastoral: God knew their courage was new and fragile. War would have broken it and sent them running back to their chains.
13.18: So God guided them on the long route — through wilderness, toward the Sea of Reeds. And Israel went out arrayed rank by rank for war, weapons in hand, freed slaves who had to become an army.
13.19: Moses carried Joseph's bones with him — Joseph had exacted an oath from Israel: 'When God comes for you, take me with you.' After four centuries, that old promise was honoured. The exodus carried its own dead home.
13.20: They moved out from Succoth and made camp at Etham — the last edge of the known world before the wild.
13.21: Yahweh himself led the column — cloud by day to shade and guide them, fire by night to light their path. They were never left to navigate alone; the God who freed them now walked ahead.
13.22: The cloud never lifted by day, and the fire never went out at night. Yahweh's presence was constant, visible, unbroken — a promise in the sky.

14.2: "Have Israel double back and camp before Pi-hahiroth — between Migdol and the sea, facing Baal-zephon. Set up camp right at the water's edge."
14.3: "Pharaoh will look at their position and conclude they are lost — trapped between the wilderness and the sea. Let him think that."
14.4: "I will harden Pharaoh's heart and he will chase them — and through that pursuit I will show my glory at his expense. Egypt will learn who I am." Israel obeyed.
14.5: News reached Pharaoh: the people had fled. His heart and the hearts of his officials lurched — the same hearts Yahweh had hardened. 'What have we done?' they said. 'We released our workforce. We gave away our slaves.'
14.6: Pharaoh ordered his chariot readied and marshalled his forces.
14.7: Six hundred elite war-chariots, plus the full Egyptian chariot corps — every one commanded, every one sent in pursuit.
14.8: Yahweh hardened Pharaoh's heart. Pharaoh chased. And Israel marched out with their heads high — triumphant, unashamed, free.
14.9: Egypt's full military might bore down on them: horses, chariots, cavalry, infantry — overtaking Israel just as they had stopped to camp at the sea's edge, near Pi-hahiroth.
14.10: They looked up and saw Egypt. The full army — the chariots, the dust-cloud, the closing distance. Terror swept through the camp and they cried out to Yahweh.
14.11: The people turned on Moses: 'Not enough graves in Egypt? You had to bring us out here to make new ones? What were you thinking?'
14.12: 'Didn't we tell you this back in Egypt? Leave us alone — let us keep serving. Slavery with a living body beats freedom with a dead one.' Fear had already rewritten the past.
14.13: Moses stood firm: 'Don't be afraid. Hold your ground and watch — Yahweh will deliver you today. Those Egyptians you see closing in? You will never lay eyes on them again.'
14.14: 'Yahweh fights for you. Your part is simply this: be still.'
14.15: Yahweh broke in: 'Moses — enough prayer. Tell them to go forward.'
14.16: 'Lift your staff and stretch it over the sea. Divide it. Israel will walk through on dry ground.'
14.17: 'And I will harden Egypt's hearts so they follow Israel into the sea — and I will show my glory through Pharaoh himself, through his chariots, through every soldier.'
14.18: 'Egypt will know who I am — not through theology but through catastrophe. When Pharaoh's glory sinks in the sea, mine will stand alone.'
14.19: The angel of God — the same presence that had led them — stepped out of the lead and moved to their rear. The pillar of cloud shifted to stand between Israel and Egypt.
14.20: One cloud, two effects: Egypt's side was plunged into darkness; Israel's side was bathed in light. The whole night passed and neither army could touch the other.
14.21: Moses stretched out his hand. Yahweh drove the sea back with a roaring east wind all through the night. The seabed became dry ground. The waters split apart and stood.
14.22: Israel walked through. Dry ground underfoot, walls of water on both sides — an impossible corridor opened by the God of the impossible.
14.23: Egypt followed. Every horse, every chariot, every soldier poured into the corridor after them — into the trap.
14.24: In the last watch before dawn, Yahweh looked down from the pillar of fire and cloud — and panic broke loose in the Egyptian ranks.
14.25: The chariot wheels were seized — they lurched and ground to a halt. And then even the Egyptians understood: 'Run. Yahweh is fighting for them against us.'
14.26: Yahweh commanded Moses: 'Stretch your hand over the sea. Let the waters return upon Egypt.'
14.27: Moses raised his hand. At dawn the sea rushed back to full strength. Egypt turned to run — and Yahweh hurled them into the waves.
14.28: The sea closed over everything: chariots, horses, soldiers — all of Pharaoh's army that had entered the corridor. Not one came back out.
14.29: Israel had walked through on dry ground, water standing on both sides like walls. What was death for Egypt was salvation for Israel — the same sea, the same night, two opposite outcomes.
14.30: That day Yahweh saved Israel from Egypt's grip. And in the morning, they stood on the shore and looked at what had been the most powerful army in the world — washed up, dead, finished.
14.31: They saw the scale of what Yahweh had done. And the people feared — not the fear that paralyzes, but the fear that worships. They trusted Yahweh and his servant Moses.

15.1:
Then Moses and Israel broke into song before Yahweh:
"I will sing to Yahweh — he has risen in triumph!
Horse and rider he has flung into the sea."
15.2:
"Yahweh is my strength, my song — he became my deliverance.
He is my God and I will make him a sanctuary,
my father's God — I will lift him high."
15.3: "Yahweh is a warrior — Yahweh is his name."
15.4:
"Pharaoh's chariots and his army — thrown into the sea.
His elite officers swallowed by the Sea of Reeds."
15.5:
"The deep closed over them;
they sank like stones to the bottom."
15.6:
"Your right hand, Yahweh — glorious in strength!
Your right hand, Yahweh — it shattered the enemy!"
15.7:
"In the fullness of your majesty you crushed your opponents;
you released your fury — it burned them like dry straw."
15.8:
"One breath from your nostrils — the waters heaped up.
The floods stood straight like a wall.
The deeps froze at the sea's core."
15.9:
"The enemy said:
'I will pursue, I will catch them,
I will divide the spoil — fill my hands with it,
unsheathe my sword, cut them down.'"
Words of a predator. Words that came to nothing.
15.10:
"You breathed — and the sea covered them.
They sank like lead in those vast, indifferent depths."
15.11:
"Who compares to you, Yahweh, among any so-called god?
Who matches you — holy in majesty, terrible in glory, worker of wonders?
No one. Nothing."
15.12: "You reached out your hand — the earth swallowed them whole."
15.13:
"In your covenant loyalty you have led the people you ransomed;
in your strength you have guided them toward your holy mountain."
15.14:
"The nations have heard — they shudder.
Philistia writhes in sudden dread."
15.16:
"Terror and dread fall upon them;
your arm's vast power turns them to stone —
until your people cross over, Yahweh,
until the people you redeemed with blood and wonder pass through."
15.17:
"You will bring them in and plant them on your mountain,
the place you made your own home, Yahweh —
the sanctuary your hands built and will not abandon."
15.19: Pharaoh's horses, his chariots, his cavalry — they entered the sea. Yahweh brought the sea back over them. But Israel had walked through on dry ground. The song is grounded in history; the poetry did not exaggerate.
15.20: Then Miriam the prophetess — Aaron's sister, and a leader in her own right — took up a tambourine. All the women followed her out into the dance.
15.21:
Miriam led the response:
"Sing to Yahweh — he has risen in triumph!
Horse and rider he has flung into the sea!"
15.22: Moses led Israel away from the sea into the wilderness of Shur. Three days they walked. No water. The song had barely dried on their lips.
15.23: At Marah the water was undrinkable — bitter as salt. So the place was named for it: Marah. Bitterness.
15.24: The people turned on Moses: 'What are we supposed to drink?'
15.25: Moses cried out. Yahweh showed him a tree — he threw it in, and the bitter water turned sweet. It was also the place where Yahweh set a legal standard and began to test them: could they trust him when conditions were hard?
15.26: 'If you will truly listen to Yahweh your God — do what is right, heed his commands, keep his statutes — then I will not send on you the plagues I sent on Egypt. I am Yahweh your healer.' The offer at Marah: obedience in exchange for the health Egypt lost.
15.27: Then they reached Elim — twelve springs, seventy palms, water enough for everyone. They camped beside it and rested.

16.1: The whole congregation moved out from Elim into the wilderness of Sin — halfway between Elim and Sinai — on the fifteenth day of the second month. Exactly one month since they had left Egypt.
16.2: In the wilderness the whole congregation turned against Moses and Aaron. Hunger drives short memories.
16.3: 'We should have died in Egypt — at least there we had meat, bread, enough. You brought us out here to starve the whole community.' They had exchanged slavery with food for freedom with hunger, and freedom was losing.
16.4: Yahweh said to Moses: 'I will rain bread from the sky. Each day they gather what they need for that day — no more, no less. It is a test: can they trust daily provision? Can they stop hoarding and rest in my sufficiency?'
16.5: 'On the sixth day they will prepare double — because the seventh is for rest, not gathering.'
16.6: Moses and Aaron addressed the whole company: 'This evening you will see that it was Yahweh who brought you out of Egypt.'
16.7: 'In the morning you will see Yahweh's glory — because he has heard you grumbling against him. We are nothing. Your complaint is not with us; it is with God.'
16.8: 'Yahweh will give you meat at dusk and bread at dawn — because he heard your complaint. And let me be clear: your quarrel is not with Moses and Aaron. It is with Yahweh himself.'
16.9: Moses said to Aaron: 'Call the assembly before Yahweh — he has heard them.'
16.10: As Aaron spoke, they turned and looked out toward the wilderness — and the glory of Yahweh blazed in the cloud.
16.12: 'I have heard the grumbling. Tell them: at dusk — meat. At dawn — bread enough. And through this they will come to know: I am Yahweh their God.'
16.13: That evening quail descended and blanketed the camp. By morning, dew lay all around — and when it lifted, something was left on the ground.
16.14: When the dew evaporated, a thin flaky substance lay over the ground — fine as frost, delicate as scales. Something no one had seen before.
16.15: 'What is it?' — they asked each other. The Hebrew word stuck: man hu, 'what is it?' — and the name manna was born from the question. Moses answered: 'This is the bread Yahweh has given you.'
16.16: 'Yahweh's instruction: gather an omer per person — one day's portion per head, for everyone in your tent. No more, no less.'
16.17: They gathered — some more, some less, each according to their energy and their fear.
16.18: When they measured it out, something extraordinary happened: whoever had gathered much found only an omer; whoever had gathered little had a full omer. The provision was self-equalising. No one had too much; no one had too little.
16.19: 'Leave none of it till morning,' Moses said.
16.20: Some didn't listen. They kept a supply overnight — and by morning it was writhing with maggots and reeked. Moses was furious. The provision was not meant to be hoarded; it was meant to be trusted.
16.21: Each morning they gathered it — each person what they needed. When the sun climbed, it melted. Each day a fresh start, a fresh provision, a fresh act of trust.
16.22: On the sixth day the gathering doubled naturally — two omers a head. The community leaders came to Moses, puzzled.
16.23: 'Yahweh said: Tomorrow is Sabbath — a holy rest. Cook what you want today. What remains, set aside. It will keep. The Sabbath is sacred; it needs no bread-gathering to validate it.'
16.24: They set the double portion aside. In the morning it was unchanged — no rot, no worms. The Sabbath provision played by different rules.
16.25: 'Eat it today,' Moses told them. 'Today is Sabbath — Yahweh's rest. There is nothing in the field today. The ground itself is resting.'
16.26: 'Six days — gather. The seventh day is Sabbath. There will be nothing to find. The pattern is built into the provision.'
16.27: Some went out on the seventh day anyway. They found nothing. Distrust costs you the rest.
16.28: Yahweh said to Moses: 'How long will they refuse to trust me and keep my instructions?'
16.29: 'Understand: Yahweh has given you the Sabbath. That is why the sixth-day provision is doubled. Stay home on the seventh day. The Sabbath is a gift. Stop treating it as an inconvenience.'
16.31: Israel named it manna — 'What is it?' — a daily reminder that the provision came before the understanding. It was white, flaky, fine like coriander seed, sweet as honey wafers.
16.32: 'Yahweh commands: keep an omer of it for all your generations — so that your descendants can see with their own eyes the bread their ancestors ate in the wilderness when Yahweh brought them out of Egypt.'
16.33: Moses told Aaron: 'Take a jar, fill it with an omer of manna, and set it before Yahweh as a memorial for all future generations.'
16.34: Aaron did as Yahweh had commanded — he placed the jar before the Testimony, where it would remain as a witness.
16.35: Forty years. Manna every morning for forty years, until they reached land that could grow grain. The provision outlasted every doubt, every failure, every rebellion — until they came to the edge of Canaan.
16.36: An omer is one-tenth of an ephah — a day's portion, small enough to remind you daily that you depend on God.

17.1: The whole congregation moved on from the wilderness of Sin in stages — Yahweh directed each step. They camped at Rephidim. There was no water.
17.2: The people quarrelled with Moses: 'Give us water.' Moses fired back: 'Why are you fighting with me? Why are you testing Yahweh?'
17.3: But thirst overwhelmed reason. They turned on Moses again: 'Why did you bring us out of Egypt? To kill us — our children, our animals — with thirst?'
17.4: Moses cried out to Yahweh: 'What do I do with these people? They are one step from stoning me.'
17.5: Yahweh answered: 'Walk out ahead of them. Bring elders as witnesses. Carry the staff that split the Nile. Go.'
17.6: 'I will stand on the rock at Horeb. Strike it. Water will flow.' Moses obeyed before the elders — witnesses to the moment. The rock that needed striking was not abandoned; God stood on it.
17.7: He named it Massah — Testing — and Meribah — Quarrelling. Because Israel had asked the question that lay beneath all their grumbling: 'Is Yahweh actually here?' The question itself was a failure of faith. But the water flowed anyway.
17.8: Then Amalek struck — an attack on Israel at Rephidim. The first military conflict of the wilderness years.
17.9: Moses turned to Joshua: 'Select your fighters and engage Amalek. Tomorrow I will stand on the hill with the staff of God.' Leadership in battle took two forms here: Joshua in the field and Moses on the height.
17.10: Joshua took the field. Moses, Aaron, and Hur climbed to the summit.
17.11: When Moses raised his hands, Israel advanced. When his arms dropped, Amalek pushed forward. The battle below was linked to the posture above. Prayer and warfare were not separate.
17.12: Moses' arms grew heavy. Aaron and Hur set a stone for him to sit on and each took one arm and held it up. Two men held up the man who held up Israel. His arms stayed steady until the sun went down.
17.13: Joshua routed Amalek with the sword.
17.14: Yahweh commanded: 'Write this down and read it to Joshua: I will utterly erase the memory of Amalek from under heaven.' The judgment was decreed before the next battle began.
17.15: Moses built an altar and named it: Yahweh-Nissi — Yahweh is my banner. Victory did not belong to Joshua's sword; it belonged to the one who sustained Moses' arms.
17.16: He said: 'His throne is pledged to this — Yahweh will be at war with Amalek age after age.' Not merely a battle but a long covenantal conflict. The altar was a declaration, not a celebration.

18.1: Jethro, priest of Midian and Moses' father-in-law, heard the whole story — what God had done for Moses and for Israel, how Yahweh had brought them out of Egypt. The news of the exodus had reached Midian.
18.2: Moses had sent Zipporah home earlier — away from the dangerous confrontation with Pharaoh. Now Jethro brought her back to him.
18.3: and her two sons. The first was named Gershom — 'a stranger there' — because Moses had said when the boy was born: 'I have been an alien in a foreign land.'
18.4: The second was Eliezer — 'my God is help' — because Moses had said at his birth: 'My father's God was my help and delivered me from Pharaoh's sword.' Both names were theology. Both names were autobiography.
18.5: Jethro arrived at the mountain of God — the same mountain where Yahweh had first spoken to Moses from the burning bush — bringing Zipporah and the boys.
18.6: He sent a message ahead: 'I — Jethro, your father-in-law — am coming. Your wife and sons are with me.'
18.7: Moses went out to meet him — bowed, embraced, kissed him. They exchanged questions about each other's welfare, then went inside. No ceremony; just reunion.
18.8: Moses told the whole story — what Yahweh had done to Pharaoh, to Egypt, for Israel's sake; the hardships along the way; the deliverances that had punctuated the journey. He held nothing back.
18.9: Jethro was filled with joy at the goodness Yahweh had shown Israel — how he had rescued them from Egypt's grip. The outsider rejoiced in Israel's liberation.
18.10: Jethro said: 'Blessed be Yahweh, who rescued you from Egypt and from Pharaoh — who delivered his people from under Egypt's crushing hand.' A Midianite priest worshipping Israel's God.
18.11: 'Now I know — I know — that Yahweh towers above every god. In the very thing Egypt used to oppress, Yahweh used to judge. Their pride was their undoing; his honour was vindicated.' Jethro had come to a conclusion. It was a conversion.
18.12: Jethro brought burnt offerings and sacrifices to God. Aaron and all the elders of Israel came and shared a covenant meal with Moses' father-in-law in the presence of God. An outsider's table became holy ground.
18.13: The next morning Moses sat to judge the people — and they queued from dawn to dusk. The line never ended.
18.14: Jethro watched all day. Then he asked: 'What are you doing? Why are you alone in the seat while all these people wait from sunrise to sunset?'
18.15: Moses explained: 'They come to me to inquire of God.'
18.16: 'When there is a dispute they bring it to me. I decide between them and teach them God's statutes and laws.' A right answer. But not a sustainable one.
18.17: Jethro didn't soften it: 'What you are doing is not good.'
18.18: 'You will exhaust yourself — and the people too. This is too heavy for one man. You cannot carry it alone.'
18.19: 'Listen to me — I offer counsel, and may God confirm it. You should remain the people's representative before God — the one who brings the great cases to him.'
18.20: 'Teach them the statutes and laws. Show them the path to walk and what their work looks like. Give them the principles so they can apply them.'
18.21: 'But this: find capable men — men who fear God, who are truthful, who despise bribes — and appoint them as leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens. Distribute authority. What you cannot carry alone, share.'
18.22: 'Let them judge the routine cases. Only the large and difficult ones come to you. The burden will be shared and you will last. And so will they.'
18.23: 'If you do this — and if God confirms it — you will endure. And all these people will go home in peace instead of waiting in exhaustion.'
18.24: Moses listened. He implemented everything Jethro had said. Wisdom does not require a prophet; it can come from a Midianite father-in-law.
18.25: Moses chose capable men from across Israel and appointed them leaders: over thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens. A structure was built.
18.26: They handled the everyday disputes. The hard cases came to Moses. The system worked because the responsibility was distributed.
18.27: Moses said goodbye to Jethro, and his father-in-law went home to Midian. He had brought Moses' family back, shared a sacrifice, given counsel that would shape Israel's governance for generations, and departed. A brief visit; a lasting legacy.

1.1: These are the names of Jacob's sons who entered Egypt—each man arriving with his own household.
1.4: Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.
1.5: Seventy of Jacob's own descendants made the journey—but Joseph was already in Egypt.
1.6: Joseph died. His brothers died. That whole generation passed away.
1.7: But the Israelites were fruitful and swarmed, multiplying with extraordinary vigor until the whole land of Egypt was teeming with them.
1.8: Then a new king came to power in Egypt—one who knew nothing of Joseph.
1.9: 'Look,' he said to his people, 'these Israelites are becoming too numerous and too powerful for us.'
1.10: 'We have to act shrewdly—if we let them keep multiplying, the day war comes they will side with our enemies, fight against us, and walk out of the country.'
1.11: The Egyptians put slave masters over them to crush them under forced labor. With that labor they built the storage cities of Pithom and Rameses for Pharaoh.
1.12: But the harder they oppressed them, the more the Israelites multiplied and spread—until the Egyptians were filled with dread at the sight of them.
1.13: The Egyptians drove the Israelites into brutal forced labor.
1.14: Life became bitter—endless toil in mortar and brick, backbreaking fieldwork of every kind. Every form of their labor was grinding and merciless.
1.15: The king of Egypt gave orders to the Hebrew midwives—Shiphrah and Puah were their names—
1.16: with this command: 'When you assist the Hebrew women in childbirth, if the child is a boy, kill him on the spot. If it is a girl, let her live.'
1.17: But the midwives feared God. They refused to obey the king's command and kept the boys alive.
1.18: Pharaoh summoned the midwives. 'Why have you done this?' he demanded. 'Why have you let the boys live?'
1.19: The midwives told Pharaoh, 'Hebrew women are not like Egyptian women—they are strong and give birth before we can even get there.'
1.20: God rewarded the midwives. The Israelite people kept multiplying and grew mightier still.
1.21: Because the midwives feared God, he established families for them.
1.22: Pharaoh issued an order to all his people: 'Every Hebrew boy who is born must be thrown into the Nile. Every girl you may let live.'

2.1: A man of the tribe of Levi took a woman of his own tribe as his wife.
2.2: She conceived and gave birth to a son. When she saw what a beautiful child he was, she hid him for three months.
2.3: When she could no longer hide him, she wove a basket of papyrus reeds, sealed it with tar and pitch, laid the baby inside, and set it floating among the reeds at the river's edge.
2.4: His sister took up a position some distance away to watch what would happen to him.
2.5: Pharaoh's daughter came down to bathe in the Nile while her attendants walked along the bank. She spotted the basket among the reeds and sent her servant girl to fetch it.
2.6: She opened it and found the baby—he was crying. Her heart went out to him. 'This must be one of the Hebrew children,' she said.
2.7: The baby's sister stepped forward and said to Pharaoh's daughter, 'Shall I go and find a Hebrew woman to nurse the baby for you?'
2.8: 'Go,' said Pharaoh's daughter. The girl went and brought the child's own mother.
2.9: Pharaoh's daughter said to her, 'Take this baby and nurse him for me. I will pay you.' So the woman took her own child and nursed him.
2.10: The child grew, and she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, who adopted him as her own son. She named him Moses—'Because,' she said, 'I drew him out of the water.'
2.11: When Moses was grown, he went out to his own people and watched them struggling under their burdens. He saw an Egyptian beating a Hebrew—one of his own kinsmen.
2.12: Moses glanced both ways; seeing no one, he killed the Egyptian and buried the body in the sand.
2.13: The next day he went out again and found two Hebrews brawling. He turned to the one who had started it: 'Why are you hitting your own countryman?'
2.14: 'Who appointed you as our ruler and judge?' the man shot back. 'Are you planning to kill me the way you killed that Egyptian?' Moses was gripped with fear. The matter had gotten out.
2.15: When Pharaoh heard what had happened, he sought to kill Moses. Moses fled from Pharaoh and settled in Midian. There he sat down by a well.
2.16: The priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came to the well, drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flocks.
2.17: Shepherds came and drove them off, but Moses stood up, intervened on their behalf, and watered the flocks himself.
2.18: When they returned to their father Reuel, he asked, 'Why are you back so early today?'
2.19: 'An Egyptian man rescued us from the shepherds,' they told him. 'He even drew water for us and watered the flocks.'
2.20: 'Where is he?' Reuel asked his daughters. 'Why did you leave him there? Invite him in for a meal.'
2.21: Moses was content to settle with him, and Reuel gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses as his wife.
2.22: She bore a son, and Moses named him Gershom—'Sojourner there'—for he said, 'I have been a stranger in a foreign land.'
2.23: A long time passed. The king of Egypt died. The Israelites were still groaning under their bondage; they cried out, and their cry from slavery ascended to God.
2.24: God heard their groaning. God remembered the covenant he had made with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.
2.25: God saw the Israelites. God took notice.

3.1: Moses was tending the flock of his father-in-law Jethro, priest of Midian, when he led the sheep to the far side of the desert and came to Horeb—the mountain of God.
3.2: There the angel of the LORD appeared to him—a flame of fire blazing from the heart of a thornbush. Moses looked: the bush was burning, yet the bush was not being consumed.
3.3: Moses said to himself, 'Let me step aside and look at this remarkable sight—why isn't the bush burning up?'
3.4: When the LORD saw that Moses had turned aside to look, God called to him from the heart of the bush: 'Moses! Moses!' 'Here I am,' Moses answered.
3.5: 'Come no closer,' God said. 'Take off your sandals—the ground where you stand is holy ground.'
3.6: Then he said, 'I am the God of your father—the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob.' Moses covered his face, afraid to look at God.
3.7: 'I have seen the suffering of my people in Egypt,' the LORD said. 'I have heard their cry under the taskmasters. I know what they are enduring.'
3.8: 'So I have come down to rescue them from Egypt's grip, to bring them up out of that land into a good and wide land—a land overflowing with milk and honey—the territory now occupied by the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.'
3.9: 'The cry of the Israelites has reached me—I have seen how the Egyptians are crushing them.'
3.10: 'So go—I am sending you to Pharaoh. Bring my people, the Israelites, out of Egypt.'
3.11: But Moses said to God, 'Who am I to go to Pharaoh? Who am I to lead the Israelites out of Egypt?'
3.12: 'I will be with you,' God said. 'And this is how you will know I sent you: when you have brought the people out of Egypt, you will worship God right here on this mountain.'
3.13: Moses said to God, 'Suppose I go to the Israelites and tell them, "The God of your fathers has sent me." And they ask, "What is his name?"—what do I say?'
3.14: God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM—and I will be what I will be.' Then he said, 'Tell the Israelites: "I AM has sent me to you."'
3.15: God said further, 'Tell the Israelites: "Yahweh—the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—has sent me to you." This is my name forever. This is how I am to be remembered in every generation.'
3.16: 'Go and assemble the elders of Israel. Tell them: "The LORD—the God of your fathers, Abraham's God, Isaac's God, Jacob's God—has appeared to me. He says: I have seen what Egypt has done to you, and I have come."'
3.17: 'I have promised to bring you up from Egypt's misery into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites—a land flowing with milk and honey.'
3.18: 'The elders will listen to you. Then you and the elders are to go to Pharaoh and say: "The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has appeared to us. Please let us go three days' journey into the desert to offer sacrifice to the LORD our God."'
3.19: 'I know Pharaoh well—he will not let you go unless forced by a mighty hand.'
3.20: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with wonder after wonder. After that, Pharaoh will release you.'
3.21: 'I will cause the Egyptians to look favorably on my people, so that when you leave, you will not go out empty-handed.'
3.22: 'Every Israelite woman is to ask her Egyptian neighbor for jewelry of silver and gold and for fine clothing. You will dress your sons and daughters in them—and strip Egypt clean.'

4.1: Moses objected: 'But what if they don't believe me—if they refuse to listen and say, "The LORD never appeared to you"?'
4.2: 'What is that in your hand?' the LORD asked. 'A staff,' Moses said.
4.3: 'Throw it on the ground,' the LORD said. Moses threw it down, and it became a snake. Moses ran from it.
4.4: 'Reach out and take it by the tail,' the LORD told him. Moses reached out and grabbed it, and it became a staff in his hand again.
4.5: 'That will convince them that the LORD has appeared to you—the God of their fathers, Abraham's God, Isaac's God, Jacob's God.'
4.6: Then the LORD said, 'Now put your hand inside your cloak.' He did—and when he withdrew it, the hand was covered with disease, white as snow.
4.7: 'Put it back,' the LORD said. He did—and when he withdrew it again, the hand was fully restored, like the rest of his skin.
4.8: 'If they are not convinced by the first sign, the second should convince them.'
4.9: 'If they still refuse to believe after both signs, here is what you do: take water from the Nile and pour it on dry ground. The Nile water will become blood the moment it hits the earth.'
4.10: 'My Lord,' Moses said, 'I have never been a man of words—not before and not now that you have spoken to me. I am slow and inarticulate.'
4.11: 'Who made the human mouth?' the LORD replied. 'Who makes someone deaf or mute, sighted or blind? Is it not I, the LORD?'
4.12: 'Now go—I will be with your mouth and teach you what to say.'
4.13: 'Please, my Lord,' Moses said. 'Send someone else.'
4.14: The LORD's anger flared at Moses. 'Your brother Aaron the Levite—he can certainly speak. And right now he is on his way to meet you, and when he sees you, his heart will be glad.'
4.15: 'You will speak to him and put the words in his mouth. I will be with both your mouths and teach you what to do.'
4.16: 'He will be your mouthpiece to the people, the way you speak for God to him.'
4.17: 'Take this staff in your hand—it is the instrument you will use to perform the signs.'
4.18: Moses went back to his father-in-law Jethro. 'Let me return to my kinsmen in Egypt,' he said, 'and see if they are still alive.' Jethro said, 'Go with my blessing.'
4.19: The LORD had told Moses in Midian, 'Go back to Egypt—all those who wanted you dead are gone.'
4.20: Moses loaded his wife and sons on a donkey and headed back to Egypt, with the staff of God in his hand.
4.21: 'When you arrive in Egypt,' the LORD told Moses, 'perform all the wonders I have given you before Pharaoh. But I myself will harden his heart so that he will not let the people go.'
4.22: 'Then tell Pharaoh: "This is what the LORD says: Israel is my son, my firstborn."'
4.23: 'I am saying to you: Let my son go, so he can worship me. If you refuse to let him go, I will put your firstborn son to death.'
4.24: On the road, at the lodging place, the LORD confronted Moses and was about to kill him.
4.25: Zipporah grabbed a sharp stone, cut off her son's foreskin, and threw it at Moses' feet. 'You are a bridegroom of blood to me!' she cried.
4.26: Then God let him go. Zipporah's words—'a bridegroom of blood'—referred to the circumcision.
4.27: The LORD told Aaron, 'Go into the desert to meet Moses.' Aaron went and met him at the mountain of God. They embraced.
4.28: Moses told Aaron everything the LORD had said when he sent him, and all the signs he had commissioned him to perform.
4.29: Moses and Aaron assembled all the elders of Israel.
4.30: Aaron spoke everything the LORD had told Moses, then performed the signs before the people.
4.31: The people believed. When they heard that the LORD had taken notice of them and had seen their suffering, they bowed their heads and worshipped.

5.1: Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and said, 'This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, declares: "Let my people go, so they can celebrate a festival to me in the desert."'
5.2: Pharaoh said, 'Who is this LORD that I should obey him and let Israel go? I don't know this LORD, and I will not let Israel go.'
5.3: 'The God of the Hebrews has appeared to us,' they said. 'Please let us take a three-day journey into the desert and offer sacrifice to the LORD our God—otherwise he may strike us with plague or the sword.'
5.4: The king of Egypt said, 'Moses and Aaron, why are you pulling the people away from their work? Get back to your labor!'
5.5: Pharaoh said, 'There are so many of these people—and you are giving them excuses to stop working!'
5.6: That very day Pharaoh issued orders to the slave drivers and foremen:
5.7: 'Stop supplying the people with straw for making bricks. Make them go find their own.'
5.8: 'But require the same quota of bricks as before—not one less. They are lazy, which is why they keep saying, "Let us go and offer sacrifice to our God."'
5.9: 'Pile on the work—keep them so busy they don't have time to listen to Moses' empty words.'
5.10: The slave drivers and foremen went out to the people and announced, 'Pharaoh's orders: no more straw.'
5.11: 'Go and find straw wherever you can—but your quota is not going down by one brick.'
5.12: The people scattered across Egypt, picking up stubble to use in place of straw.
5.13: The slave drivers pressed them relentlessly: 'Meet your daily quota—same as when straw was provided.'
5.14: The Israelite foremen whom Pharaoh's slave drivers had appointed were beaten. 'Why haven't you met your brick quota, the same as before?' they were demanded.
5.15: The Israelite foremen went to Pharaoh and cried out, 'Why are you treating your servants this way?'
5.16: 'No straw has been given to us, yet they demand: "Make bricks!" Your servants are being beaten, and the blame lies squarely with your own officials.'
5.17: Pharaoh said, 'Idle! You are idle! That is why you keep whining, "Let us go and offer sacrifice to the LORD."'
5.18: 'Get back to work. No straw, same quota—that's final.'
5.19: The Israelite foremen understood the trouble they were in when they were told, 'You will not reduce the daily brick quota by even one.'
5.20: Coming out from Pharaoh's presence, they ran into Moses and Aaron who were waiting for them.
5.21: 'May the LORD see what you have done and judge you for it! You have made us repugnant to Pharaoh and his officials—you have put a sword in their hands to kill us.'
5.22: Moses went back to the LORD. 'My Lord,' he said, 'why have you brought disaster on this people? Is this what you sent me for?'
5.23: 'Since I first went to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has only made things worse for these people—and you have done nothing to rescue them.'

6.1: The LORD said to Moses, 'Now you will see what I am going to do to Pharaoh. My strong hand will compel him—not merely let them go, but drive them out.'
6.2: God spoke to Moses and said, 'I am Yahweh—the LORD.'
6.3: 'I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as God Almighty—El Shaddai—but my personal name, Yahweh, I did not reveal to them in its full significance.'
6.4: 'I also made a covenant with them to give them the land of Canaan—the land where they had lived as aliens.'
6.5: 'I have heard the Israelites groaning under Egypt's boot—and I have remembered my covenant.'
6.6: 'So tell the Israelites: "I am the LORD. I will bring you out from under Egypt's burdens. I will free you from their slavery. I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and mighty acts of judgment."'
6.7: 'I will claim you as my people, and I will be your God. Then you will know that I—the LORD—am your God, the one who brought you out from under Egypt's burdens.'
6.8: 'I will bring you into the land I promised with a raised hand to give to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. It will be your inheritance. I am the LORD.'
6.9: Moses told this to the Israelites, but they could not take it in. Their spirit was crushed, broken by years of brutal slavery.
6.11: 'Go! Tell Pharaoh king of Egypt to release the Israelites from his land.'
6.12: Moses appealed to the LORD: 'If the Israelites won't listen to me, why would Pharaoh? I'm no speaker—my lips are faltering.'
6.13: But the LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron and commissioned them to bring the Israelites out of Egypt—dealing with both the Israelites and Pharaoh king of Egypt.
6.14: These are the heads of the ancestral houses. Reuben, Israel's firstborn, had four sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi—these are the clans of Reuben.
6.15: Simeon's sons: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman—these are the clans of Simeon.
6.16: The sons of Levi, in family sequence: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari. Levi lived 137 years.
6.17: Gershon's sons: Libni and Shimei, according to their clans.
6.18: Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years.
6.19: Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of Levi in sequence of descent.
6.20: Amram married Jochebed, his father's sister. She bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years.
6.21: Izhar's sons: Korah, Nepheg, and Zichri.
6.22: Uzziel's sons: Mishael, Elzaphan, and Sithri.
6.23: Aaron married Elisheba—daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon—and she bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
6.24: Korah's sons: Assir, Elkanah, and Abiasaph—these are the clans of the Korahites.
6.25: Aaron's son Eleazar married a daughter of Putiel, and she bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the Levite ancestral clans.
6.26: These were the Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said, 'Bring the Israelites out of Egypt, division by division.'
6.27: They were the ones who confronted Pharaoh king of Egypt and demanded the release of the Israelites—this same Moses and Aaron.
6.28: On the day the LORD spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,
6.29: the LORD said to Moses, 'I am the LORD. Speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt everything I say to you.'
6.30: But Moses said to the LORD, 'My lips are faltering and inarticulate—how will Pharaoh take me seriously?'

7.1: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Pay attention: I have set you in the place of God before Pharaoh — his authority finds its limit in you — and Aaron your brother will speak as your prophet.'
7.2: You will say every word I give you, and Aaron will relay it to Pharaoh — that he must release the children of Israel from his land.
7.3: I will make Pharaoh's heart stubborn, and I will multiply my signs and portents throughout the land of Egypt.
7.4: Pharaoh will not listen. Then I will stretch my hand over Egypt and march out my armies — my own people, the children of Israel — from Egypt with sweeping acts of judgment.
7.5: When I reach my hand against Egypt and lead Israel out from their midst, Egypt will be forced to acknowledge: I am Yahweh.
7.6: Moses and Aaron carried out everything Yahweh had commanded — without deviation.
7.7: Moses was eighty and Aaron eighty-three when they stood before Pharaoh — old men carrying a young God's commission.
7.8: Yahweh spoke to both Moses and Aaron:
7.9: 'When Pharaoh demands a sign from you, tell Aaron to throw down his staff before Pharaoh — it will become a serpent.'
7.10: Moses and Aaron came before Pharaoh and did as Yahweh had commanded. Aaron threw his staff to the ground before Pharaoh and his entire court — and it became a serpent.
7.11: Pharaoh called in his wise men and sorcerers — Egypt's own magicians — and they duplicated the feat with their secret arts, each throwing down a staff that became a serpent.
7.12: Every magician's staff became a serpent — but Aaron's serpent devoured them all.
7.13: Yet Pharaoh's heart remained rigid; he would not listen — exactly as Yahweh had foretold.
7.14: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Pharaoh's heart is stone-heavy; he will not release my people.'
7.15: Go to Pharaoh at dawn — he goes out to the Nile. Station yourself on the riverbank to confront him, holding in your hand the staff that became a serpent.
7.16: Tell him: Yahweh, the God of the Hebrews, has sent me to you. His demand stands: Release my people so they can worship me in the desert. And still you have not listened.
7.17: Yahweh's word to you: Here is how you will know that I am Yahweh — I will strike the waters of the Nile with this staff, and they will turn to blood.
7.18: The fish will die, the river will reek, and Egypt will gag at the very water they live by.
7.19: Yahweh told Moses to instruct Aaron: Extend your staff over all of Egypt's waters — the Nile, canals, ponds, every reservoir — and they will become blood. Blood will saturate the entire land, even the water stored in wooden and stone vessels.
7.20: Moses and Aaron obeyed Yahweh's command. Aaron raised the staff and struck the Nile in plain sight of Pharaoh and his court — and the entire river ran with blood.
7.21: The fish died. The river reeked. Egypt could not drink from the Nile. Blood had spread through the whole land.
7.22: The Egyptian magicians performed the same act with their secret arts — a hollow triumph over waters already ruined. Pharaoh's heart stayed rigid; he would not listen, exactly as Yahweh had said.
7.23: Pharaoh turned his back and walked into his palace, his heart untouched by the ruined river.
7.24: All across Egypt people dug along the riverbank, searching for water they could actually drink — the Nile itself was useless.
7.25: Seven days went by from the time Yahweh had struck the Nile.

8.1: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Go back to Pharaoh and deliver this: Yahweh demands it — release my people so they can worship me.'
8.2: If you still refuse, I will send frogs swarming across every inch of your land.
8.3: The Nile will vomit frogs. They will crawl into your palace, invade your bedroom, climb onto your bed, overrun your servants' quarters, infest your people's homes — even your ovens and bread bowls will be thick with them.
8.4: Frogs will crawl on you personally, on your people, on every one of your courtiers.
8.5: Yahweh told Moses to instruct Aaron: Extend your staff over Egypt's rivers, canals, and ponds — command the frogs up onto the land.
8.6: Aaron extended his hand over Egypt's waters — and frogs came up and blanketed the land.
8.7: The magicians matched them with their occult arts — producing yet more frogs on an already smothered land.
8.8: Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. 'Pray to the LORD,' he said, 'and get rid of these frogs. Then I will let the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.'
8.9: Moses replied, 'Name the hour — when would you like me to pray for you, your court, and your people, that the frogs be driven back to the Nile alone? The choice is yours.'
8.10: 'Tomorrow,' said Pharaoh. Moses answered, 'Done — so that you will know there is no one like Yahweh our God.'
8.11: The frogs will withdraw from you, your palace, your officials, and your people — retreating to the Nile alone.
8.12: Moses and Aaron left Pharaoh's presence, and Moses called out to Yahweh about the frogs he had unleashed on Egypt.
8.13: Yahweh answered Moses' prayer. Frogs died everywhere — in the houses, the yards, the open fields.
8.14: The Egyptians heaped the dead frogs into mounds — and the whole land stank.
8.15: The moment Pharaoh had breathing room, his heart turned to stone again. He refused to listen — exactly as Yahweh had said.
8.16: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Tell Aaron to strike the dust of the earth with his staff — it will swarm into gnats across the entire land of Egypt.'
8.17: Aaron raised his staff and struck the earth — and all the dust of Egypt became gnats, swarming on humans and animals alike, everywhere.
8.18: The magicians attempted to replicate this — and failed. Their power had reached its limit. Gnats covered people and animals with no remedy.
8.19: The magicians admitted to Pharaoh: 'This is the finger of God.' Even so, Pharaoh's heart stayed rigid and he would not listen — as Yahweh had said.
8.20: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Be up early — go meet Pharaoh when he goes to the Nile. Give him this: Yahweh's demand stands — release my people so they can worship me.'
8.21: If you will not let them go, I will unleash swarms of flies on you, your court, your people, and their homes. Egypt will drown in flies — on people, in houses, covering the very ground.
8.22: But Goshen — where my people dwell — will be exempt. Not a single fly will land there. This distinction will prove to you that I, Yahweh, am at work right here in the middle of this land.
8.23: I am drawing a clear line between my people and yours. Tomorrow this sign will appear.
8.24: Yahweh delivered. Thick swarms of flies flooded into Pharaoh's palace, his courtiers' homes, and all across Egypt — the land was devastated by the infestation.
8.25: Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron in. 'Fine,' he said, 'go sacrifice to your God — but stay within this land.'
8.26: Moses refused: 'That cannot work. The animals we must sacrifice to Yahweh our God are held sacred by the Egyptians — if we slaughter them before their eyes, they will stone us.'
8.27: We must travel three days into the desert to sacrifice to Yahweh our God as he directs us — no other arrangement will do.
8.28: Pharaoh relented: 'I will let you go sacrifice to Yahweh your God in the desert — but not far. And pray for me.'
8.29: 'When I walk out of here,' Moses said, 'I will pray to Yahweh — and tomorrow the flies will leave you, your court, and your people. But Pharaoh must stop playing games. No more breaking your word about letting the people go.'
8.30: Moses went out from Pharaoh's presence and prayed to Yahweh.
8.31: Yahweh answered. Every fly was removed from Pharaoh, his court, and all his people. Not one remained.
8.32: But Pharaoh hardened his heart again. This time too he refused to let the people go.

9.1: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Go back to Pharaoh. This is Yahweh the God of the Hebrews speaking: Release my people so they can worship me.'
9.2: If you refuse again and keep holding them back,
9.3: the hand of Yahweh will fall on your field animals — your horses, donkeys, camels, oxen, and flocks — with a devastating plague.
9.4: But Yahweh will draw a line between Israel's animals and Egypt's. Not one belonging to Israel will die.
9.5: Yahweh set the hour: 'Tomorrow I will do this across the land.'
9.6: The next day Yahweh did it. Every animal Egypt owned in the field was dead — but among Israel's herds, not one had fallen.
9.7: Pharaoh sent to verify — and confirmed: not one Israelite animal was dead. Even then his heart stayed hard, and he would not let the people go.
9.8: Yahweh told Moses and Aaron: 'Take handfuls of furnace soot and let Moses throw it toward the sky in Pharaoh's presence.'
9.9: It will scatter as fine dust over all Egypt and break out as festering sores on people and animals across the whole land.
9.10: They gathered the soot, stood before Pharaoh, and Moses flung it skyward — and festering boils erupted on people and animals throughout Egypt.
9.11: The magicians could not even appear before Moses — they were covered in sores like everyone else. Egypt's occult professionals were helpless.
9.12: Yahweh himself hardened Pharaoh's heart — and Pharaoh would not listen, exactly as Yahweh had told Moses would happen.
9.13: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Be up early and station yourself before Pharaoh. This is Yahweh the God of the Hebrews: Let my people go to worship me.'
9.14: This time I am sending the full force of my judgments against you personally, your court, and your people — so you will know that there is no one like me anywhere on earth.
9.15: I could already have reached out and struck you and your people with a plague that erased you from the earth.
9.16: But this is why I have let you stand — to display my power through you, so that my name would be proclaimed across the whole earth.
9.17: And still you set yourself up against my people. Still you refuse to release them.
9.18: Tomorrow at this very hour I will unleash hail the likes of which Egypt has never seen — from its founding down to this moment.
9.19: Send word now: bring every animal and every person in from the open fields. Anything left unprotected when the hail falls will die.
9.20: The officials who took Yahweh's warning seriously brought their servants and animals indoors without delay.
9.21: Those who dismissed Yahweh's word left their people and animals in the open.
9.22: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Raise your hand toward the sky — hail will fall across all Egypt, on people, on animals, on every plant growing in the open field.'
9.23: Moses lifted his staff skyward — and Yahweh unleashed thunder, hail, and lightning searing across the ground. Yahweh rained hail on the land of Egypt.
9.24: Hail and lightning struck together — a violent, unprecedented storm. Nothing like it had ever fallen on Egypt in all its history.
9.25: The hail struck everything in the open across Egypt — people, animals, crops, trees. Nothing standing in the field was spared.
9.26: Only in Goshen — where Israel lived — did no hail fall.
9.27: Pharaoh sent for Moses and Aaron. 'I have sinned this time,' he confessed. 'Yahweh is right. I and my people are in the wrong.'
9.28: 'Pray to Yahweh — this thunder and hail have gone on long enough and it is killing us. I will release you; you won't need to stay another day.'
9.29: 'The moment I leave the city,' Moses answered, 'I will spread out my hands toward Yahweh — and the thunder and hail will cease. You will know then that the whole earth belongs to Yahweh.'
9.30: 'But I know you, Pharaoh — you and your officials still do not genuinely fear Yahweh God.'
9.31: The flax was destroyed — it was in full bloom — and the barley with it, for the barley had already formed its heads.
9.32: The wheat and spelt, however, escaped — they were still young and not yet up.
9.33: Moses left Pharaoh and walked outside the city. He spread his hands toward Yahweh — and the thunder stopped, the hail stopped, the rain ceased.
9.34: The moment Pharaoh saw the storm was over, he sinned again. He and his officials hardened their hearts once more.
9.35: Pharaoh's heart stayed stone-hard — he would not let the children of Israel go. It was exactly as Yahweh had told Moses.

10.1: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Go to Pharaoh. I have hardened his heart and his officials' hearts so that I could display these signs of mine among them.'
10.2: And so that you will have this story to tell — your children and grandchildren will hear how I dealt with Egypt, what signs I showed there. That is how every generation will know: I am Yahweh.
10.3: Moses and Aaron came before Pharaoh. 'This is Yahweh the God of the Hebrews speaking: How long will you refuse to bow before me? Let my people go to worship me.'
10.4: If you refuse again, tomorrow I will bring locusts swarming into your country.
10.5: They will blanket the ground until you cannot see the earth. They will devour every plant the hail left standing — and strip every tree still growing in your fields.
10.6: They will pour into your palace, your officials' homes, every Egyptian household — the likes of which your ancestors never witnessed in all their days on earth.' Moses turned and walked out of Pharaoh's presence.
10.7: Pharaoh's own officials said to him, 'How long are you going to let this man destroy us? Let the people go to worship Yahweh their God. Can you not see that Egypt is already finished?'
10.8: Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh, who said, 'Go ahead — worship Yahweh your God. But which ones exactly are going?'
10.9: Moses answered, 'Everyone is going — young and old, sons and daughters, our flocks and herds — because we have a feast to celebrate before Yahweh.'
10.10: 'The LORD be with you indeed!' Pharaoh replied sarcastically. 'You think I'm letting you take your children? You're obviously planning far more than you've admitted.'
10.11: 'No. The men can go and worship Yahweh — that's all you asked for in the first place.' And they were expelled from Pharaoh's presence.
10.12: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Stretch your hand over Egypt — let the locusts come up and eat every plant the hail left standing.'
10.13: Moses raised his staff over Egypt. Yahweh drove an east wind across the land all day and through the night — and by morning the east wind had brought the locusts.
10.14: The locusts swept across all Egypt and settled throughout the land in massive numbers. Never before had there been such a locust invasion, and never would be again.
10.15: They darkened the earth — then devoured it. Every plant, every fruit the hail had spared. Nothing green was left on tree or ground anywhere across Egypt.
10.16: Pharaoh rushed to summon Moses and Aaron. 'I have sinned,' he said, 'against Yahweh your God and against you.'
10.17: 'Forgive my sin — just this once — and pray to Yahweh your God to take this death away from me.'
10.18: Moses went out from Pharaoh and prayed to Yahweh.
10.19: Yahweh shifted the wind — a powerful west wind swept up every locust and drove them into the Sea of Reeds. Not one remained in all of Egypt.
10.20: But Yahweh hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not release the children of Israel.
10.21: Yahweh said to Moses, 'Extend your hand toward the sky — a darkness will come over Egypt so thick it can be touched.'
10.22: Moses raised his hand toward the sky — and a thick, tangible darkness covered all Egypt for three days.
10.23: No one could see another person. No one could move from where they sat. For three days Egypt was a blind nation — but every Israelite home had light.
10.24: Pharaoh called Moses to him. 'Go, worship Yahweh — your children can go too. Just leave your herds and flocks behind.'
10.25: Moses refused: 'You must give us animals for sacrifice and burnt offering to bring to Yahweh our God.'
10.26: 'Not a single hoof stays behind — every animal goes with us. We do not yet know exactly what Yahweh will require of us until we arrive and he tells us.'
10.27: But Yahweh hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he refused.
10.28: Pharaoh said to Moses, 'Get out of my presence. Never let me see your face again. The day you appear before me, you die.'
10.29: Moses answered, 'You have spoken well. I will never see your face again.'

11.1: Yahweh had said to Moses, 'One more plague I will bring on Pharaoh and on Egypt. After that, he will let you go — and when he does, he will drive you out entirely.'
11.2: Tell the Israelites — every man and every woman — to ask their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold jewelry.
11.3: Yahweh gave the Israelites such favor in Egyptian eyes that their neighbors were glad to give. By this point Moses himself was held in high respect throughout Egypt — by Pharaoh's officials and by the common people alike.
11.4: Moses declared to Pharaoh, 'Yahweh's word is this: At midnight I will pass through the midst of Egypt.'
11.5: Every firstborn in Egypt will die — from Pharaoh's heir on the throne to the slave woman's firstborn child at her millstone, and every firstborn animal.
11.6: A cry of anguish will break out across Egypt unlike any the land has ever heard or will hear again.
11.7: But among all Israel, not even a dog will growl — no person, no animal harmed. This is how you will know that Yahweh draws a sharp line between Egypt and Israel.
11.8: All your officials will come bowing before me, begging: 'Go! Leave! Take everyone who follows you!' Only then will I go.' Moses walked out from Pharaoh's presence burning with anger.
11.9: Yahweh had already told Moses, 'Pharaoh will not listen to you — I am multiplying my wonders throughout Egypt.'
11.10: Moses and Aaron performed all these signs before Pharaoh — but Yahweh hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not release the children of Israel from his land.

12.1: In the land of Egypt, Yahweh spoke to Moses and Aaron:
12.2: This month is the beginning of months for you — the first month of your year. Your calendar is being reset by this event.
12.3: Announce this to all of Israel: on the tenth day of this month, every household is to take a lamb — one per family unit.
12.4: If a household is too small to eat a whole lamb, they are to join with their nearest neighbor — calculate how many people there are and how much each person will eat, then divide the lamb accordingly.
12.5: The animal must be without blemish — a one-year-old male from the sheep or goats.
12.6: Keep it until the fourteenth day of this month. At twilight the entire assembled community of Israel will slaughter their lambs.
12.7: Take some of the blood and mark the two doorposts and the lintel of every house where the lamb will be eaten.
12.8: That night they eat the meat — roasted over fire, not otherwise — with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.
12.9: Not raw, not boiled in water — roasted whole over fire, head, legs, and inner parts included.
12.10: Do not leave any until morning — burn whatever remains.
12.11: Eat it dressed for travel — belt fastened, sandals on, staff in hand. Eat quickly. This is Yahweh's Passover.
12.12: That night I will pass through Egypt. I will strike every firstborn — person and animal — and execute judgment against every god of Egypt. I am Yahweh.
12.13: The blood on your doorposts will be a sign marking you as mine. When I see the blood, I will pass over you — passing over in the sense of sheltering you — and the destroying plague will not touch you when I strike Egypt.
12.14: This day is to be a memorial for you. Keep it as a feast to Yahweh in every generation — a permanent ordinance for all time.
12.15: Seven days you eat only unleavened bread. On the first day you purge all leaven from your homes. Whoever eats anything leavened from day one through day seven is to be cut off from the community of Israel.
12.16: The first day is a sacred gathering, and so is the seventh. Do no ordinary work on either day — the only exception is preparing food for the people.
12.17: Keep this Feast of Unleavened Bread — for it was on this precise day that I marched my armies out of Egypt. Let this day be kept in every generation as a permanent ordinance.
12.18: In the first month: from evening on the fourteenth until evening on the twenty-first, you eat only unleavened bread.
12.19: For seven days, no leaven is to be found anywhere in your homes. Foreigner or native-born — whoever eats leaven is cut off from Israel's community.
12.20: Eat nothing leavened. In every place you live, unleavened bread only.
12.21: Moses called together all the elders of Israel. 'Go now,' he said, 'choose your Passover lambs according to your families, and slaughter them.'
12.22: Take a bundle of hyssop, dip it in the blood in the basin, and brush that blood onto the lintel and both doorposts. Not one of you is to step outside until morning.
12.23: Yahweh will pass through to strike Egypt. When he sees the blood on the lintel and doorposts, he will pass over that door, sheltering it — he will not allow the destroyer to enter your home and bring death.
12.24: Keep this observance — you and your children after you — as a permanent ordinance.
12.25: When Yahweh brings you into the land he has promised to give you, keep this service there.
12.26: When your children ask you, 'What does this rite mean?'
12.27: tell them: 'This is Yahweh's Passover offering — he passed over the houses of Israel in Egypt. When he struck Egypt, our homes were sheltered.' When the people heard this, they bowed their heads and worshiped.
12.28: The Israelites went away and carried out exactly what Yahweh had commanded Moses and Aaron.
12.29: At midnight Yahweh struck. Every firstborn in Egypt died — from Pharaoh's heir on the throne to the firstborn of the prisoner in the pit, and every firstborn animal.
12.30: Pharaoh rose in the night — he, all his court, all Egypt — and a great cry went up. There was not a house in Egypt without death in it.
12.31: That night Pharaoh sent for Moses and Aaron. 'Get up!' he said. 'Get out from among my people — you and all the Israelites! Go, worship Yahweh as you demanded.'
12.32: 'Take your flocks and herds as you said, and go — and bless me too.'
12.33: The Egyptians pressed Israel to leave at once. 'We are all dying,' they said. 'If you stay, we are all dead.'
12.34: The people grabbed their dough before it could rise, wrapped their kneading bowls in cloaks, and carried them on their shoulders — there was no time to wait.
12.35: The Israelites did what Moses had told them — they asked their Egyptian neighbors for silver and gold jewelry and for clothing.
12.36: Yahweh had given Israel such favor in Egypt's eyes that the Egyptians handed over whatever was asked. Israel plundered Egypt on the way out.
12.37: The Israelites moved out from Rameses to Succoth — some 600,000 men on foot, not counting women and children.
12.38: A diverse crowd of non-Israelites joined them — along with enormous herds of livestock, flocks and cattle alike.
12.39: From the dough they had carried out, they baked flat unleavened bread. There had been no time for yeast — they were driven out of Egypt with no opportunity to prepare provisions.
12.40: The Israelites had lived in Egypt for 430 years.
12.41: On the very day those 430 years were complete, every division of Yahweh's army marched out of Egypt.
12.42: This is a night Yahweh kept watch — a vigil to bring them out of Egypt. And so this same night is kept as a vigil by all the children of Israel in every generation.
12.43: Yahweh said to Moses and Aaron: 'These are the Passover rules. No outsider may eat it.'
12.44: A slave bought with money may eat it after circumcision.
12.45: A temporary resident or hired worker — no.
12.46: The whole meal is eaten inside one house — no meat goes outside, and not one bone is broken.
12.47: The entire community of Israel keeps this observance.
12.48: If a foreigner living among you wishes to keep Passover to Yahweh, all his males must first be circumcised. Then he may join in fully, as if native-born. No one uncircumcised may eat it.
12.49: One law — the same for the native-born and for the foreigner living among you.
12.50: All the children of Israel did as Yahweh commanded Moses and Aaron — without exception.
12.51: On that very day Yahweh led the children of Israel out of Egypt — rank by rank, division by division.

19.1: Three months to the day after Israel left Egypt, they arrived in the wilderness of Sinai.
19.2: They had set out from Rephidim, crossed into the Sinai wilderness, and made camp there at the base of the mountain.
19.3: Moses climbed the mountain toward God, and the LORD called down to him: 'Here is what you must tell the house of Jacob — declare it to the people of Israel:'
19.4: 'You saw what I did to Egypt — how I lifted you like an eagle bearing its young on outstretched wings and brought you home to me.'
19.5: 'Now then — if you will listen to my voice and keep my covenant, you will be my most precious treasure from among all the peoples of the earth. The whole earth is mine,'
19.6: 'but you — you will be a nation of royal priests, a people set apart as sacred to me.' This is the message you are to deliver to Israel.
19.7: Moses returned to the camp, gathered the elders of the people, and laid before them everything the LORD had said.
19.8: The whole assembly responded as one: 'We will do everything the LORD has said.' Moses took their answer back to God.
19.9: The LORD told Moses, 'I will come to you wrapped in thick cloud so the people can hear me speaking with you — and from then on they will always trust you.' Moses reported the people's reply to the LORD.
19.10: 'Go back to the people,' the LORD told Moses. 'Consecrate them today and tomorrow; have them wash their clothes'
19.11: 'and let them be ready for the third day — for on that day the LORD will descend on Mount Sinai in full view of all the people.'
19.12: 'Mark out a boundary around the mountain and warn the people: do not set foot on it or even touch its edge. Anyone who touches this mountain will be put to death.'
19.13: 'No one may touch the offender directly — stone them or shoot them with arrows. No living thing, human or animal, may survive the trespass. Only when the ram's horn blasts long may the people ascend.'
19.14: Moses came down from the mountain and consecrated the people. They washed their clothes
19.15: and told them, 'Be ready for the third day — and refrain from sexual relations until then.'
19.16: On the morning of the third day the mountain erupted: thunder and lightning, a dense cloud wrapped around the peak, and the blast of the ram's horn louder than anything they had ever heard. The whole camp shook with terror.
19.17: Moses led the people out of the camp to stand before God, and they assembled at the base of the mountain.
19.18: The whole of Mount Sinai was shrouded in smoke, for the LORD had descended on it in fire. Smoke poured upward like the smoke of a blast furnace, and the mountain shuddered to its roots.
19.19: The trumpet blast grew longer and more intense. Moses spoke — and the voice of God thundered back in reply.
19.20: The LORD came down to the summit of Mount Sinai and summoned Moses to the top. Moses climbed up.
19.21: 'Go back down,' the LORD said, 'and warn the people. They must not push past the boundary to glimpse me — they will die.'
19.22: Even the priests who regularly approach the LORD must consecrate themselves — otherwise the LORD will burst out against them.
19.23: Moses answered the LORD, 'But the people cannot come up to Sinai — you yourself ordered the boundaries and the consecration of the mountain.'
19.24: 'Go back down,' said the LORD, 'and bring Aaron with you when you return. But keep the priests and the people from breaking through — do not let them come up to me, or I will burst out against them.'
19.25: Moses descended and delivered the warning to the people.

20.1: Then God spoke all these words:
20.2: 'I am the LORD your God — the one who brought you out of Egypt, out of the slave house.'
20.3: 'You will have no other gods alongside me.'
20.4: 'You will make no idol for yourselves — no carved image in the shape of anything above in the sky, below on the earth, or in the waters under the earth.'
20.5: 'Do not bow to them or serve them. I, the LORD your God, am a jealous God — I hold the children accountable for their fathers' rejection of me, down to the third and fourth generation.'
20.6: 'Yet I lavish covenant loyalty on thousands who love me and live by my commands.'
20.7: 'Do not invoke the name of the LORD your God for empty or deceitful purposes — the LORD will not acquit anyone who treats his name as worthless.'
20.8: 'Keep the Sabbath day holy — guard its sacred character.'
20.9: 'Six days you will work and do all your tasks,'
20.10: 'but the seventh day belongs to the LORD your God as Sabbath rest. On that day no one does any work — not you, not your children, not your servants, not your animals, not even the immigrant staying in your community.'
20.11: 'The reason: in six days the LORD made the sky and the earth, the sea and everything in them — and on the seventh day he rested. So the LORD blessed the Sabbath and set it apart as holy.'
20.12: 'Give weight and honor to your father and your mother — this is how you will have long life in the land the LORD your God is giving you.'
20.16: 'You will not testify falsely against your neighbor.'
20.17: 'Do not crave your neighbor's house. Do not crave your neighbor's spouse, servants, animals, or anything else that belongs to your neighbor.'
20.18: The people saw it all — the lightning, the thunderclaps, the blaring trumpet, the mountain wreathed in smoke — and they shook with terror. They kept their distance
20.19: and begged Moses, 'You speak to us — we can bear that. But do not let God speak to us directly, or we will die.'
20.20: Moses told them, 'Do not be afraid. God has come to test you — to plant reverence for him so deep in you that you will not sin.'
20.21: The people stayed at the edge of the distance. Moses alone walked forward into the thick darkness where God was.
20.22: The LORD told Moses, 'Tell the people of Israel: you have witnessed this with your own eyes — I spoke to you from heaven.'
20.23: 'Do not set up gods of silver or gold to stand beside me — I will have no rivals.'
20.24: 'Build me an altar of earth and offer on it your whole burnt offerings and fellowship offerings — your sheep and cattle. Wherever I choose to make my name honored, I will come and bless you.'
20.25: 'If you build me a stone altar, use unhewn stones — the moment your iron tool touches it, you desecrate it.'
20.26: 'Do not approach my altar by steps — your dignity before me must be preserved.'

21.1: These are the rulings you shall lay before the people:
21.2: When you acquire a Hebrew bondservant, he serves six years — in the seventh he goes free, owing nothing.
21.3: If he came in unmarried, he leaves unmarried; if he had a wife when he came, she leaves with him.
21.4: But if the master provided him a wife and she bore children, the wife and children remain the master's property — the man leaves alone.
21.5: But if the servant says plainly, 'I love my master and my wife and my children — I choose not to go free,'
21.6: his master brings him before the judges and then to the door or doorpost, where he pierces the servant's ear with an awl. That man is a willing servant for life.
21.7: If a man sells his daughter as a household servant, she is not subject to the same release terms as male servants.
21.8: If she displeases the master who had designated her for himself, he must allow her family to buy her back. He may not sell her to foreigners — that would be a betrayal of the agreement.
21.9: If he designates her for his son instead, he must treat her with the rights of a daughter.
21.10: If he takes an additional wife, he must not deprive the first of food, clothing, or conjugal rights.
21.11: If he withholds any of these three things from her, she goes free — no buyback required.
21.12: Whoever strikes a person dead must be put to death.
21.13: But if he did not plan the killing — if God allowed it to happen — I will set aside a place of refuge to which the killer may flee.
21.14: But the premeditated killer — the one who planned and waited — you shall drag him away even from my altar and execute him.
21.15: Strike your father or mother — you are to be put to death.
21.16: Kidnapping a person for sale is a capital crime. So is being caught with a person known to be kidnapped.
21.17: Curse your father or your mother — you are to be put to death.
21.18: If two men fight and one injures the other with a stone or his fist — not fatally, but the injured man must take to his bed —
21.19: but recovers and walks about with his staff — the one who struck him is not liable for death. He must compensate for lost time and pay all medical expenses.
21.20: If a man beats his slave — male or female — with a rod and the slave dies from the blows, he must be punished.
21.21: However, if the slave lingers for a day or two before dying, the owner is not punished — the financial loss itself is a consequence.
21.22: If brawling men cause a pregnant woman to go into early labor — and no lasting harm results — the offender pays whatever fine the woman's husband demands, subject to the court's ruling.
21.23: But if there is lasting harm, the penalty matches the injury: life for life,
21.24: eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot —
21.25: burn answered with burn, wound with wound, bruise with bruise.
21.26: If a master strikes his slave's eye and blinds it, he must set that slave free — the eye costs the master his servant.
21.27: If a master knocks out his slave's tooth, that slave goes free — the tooth earns freedom.
21.28: If an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox is stoned and its meat may not be eaten — but the owner bears no liability.
21.29: But if the ox had a history of goring, the owner knew it, and still failed to restrain it — and it kills someone — both the ox is stoned and the owner is put to death.
21.30: If the court accepts a ransom in place of death, the owner pays whatever sum is set to redeem his life.
21.31: The same law applies whether the victim was a son or a daughter.
21.32: If the ox gores a slave, the owner pays thirty shekels of silver to the slave's master, and the ox is stoned.
21.33: If a man uncovers or digs a pit and fails to cover it, and an ox or donkey falls in —
21.34: the pit's owner must make good the loss — pay the animal's owner and keep the carcass.
21.35: If one man's ox injures another man's ox and it dies, they sell the living ox and split the money — and they split the dead ox as well.
21.36: But if the ox was a known gorer and the owner never restrained it, he must replace the animal with a live one and keep the dead one.

22.1: Steal and slaughter or sell an ox — repay five oxen. Steal and slaughter or sell a sheep — repay four sheep.
22.2: If a thief breaks in at night and is killed in the act, there is no blood guilt on the homeowner.
22.3: But if the break-in occurs in daylight and the thief is killed, the killer is guilty. The thief must make full restitution; if he cannot, he is sold to cover his debt.
22.4: If the stolen animal is found alive in his hands — ox, donkey, or sheep — he must repay double.
22.5: If a man's animal strays and eats another's crops, the owner must make it right from the best of his own field or vineyard.
22.6: If fire gets out of control and burns another man's grain stacks, standing crops, or fields, the one who set the fire must make full restitution.
22.7: If a man entrusts money or valuables to a neighbor for safekeeping and they are stolen from that neighbor's house — if the thief is caught, he repays double.
22.8: But if the thief is never caught, the neighbor must appear before God and swear he did not take the items himself.
22.9: For every disputed claim — ox, donkey, sheep, clothing, or any lost item — both sides bring the case before God. The one God finds guilty pays the other double.
22.10: If a man leaves his neighbor in charge of a donkey, ox, sheep, or any animal, and the animal dies, is injured, or disappears without any witness —
22.11: the matter is settled by an oath before the LORD: the caretaker swears he did not take the animal. The owner must accept the oath, and no restitution is required.
22.12: But if the animal was stolen on his watch, he must make good the loss.
22.13: If a wild animal killed it, the caretaker brings the carcass as proof — he owes no restitution for what a predator took.
22.14: If a man borrows an animal and it is injured or dies when the owner is not present, the borrower must make full restitution.
22.15: But if the owner was there, no restitution is required. If it was a hired animal, the hiring fee covers the risk.
22.16: If a man seduces an unbetrothed virgin and sleeps with her, he must pay the bride-price and take her as his wife.
22.17: If her father refuses, the man must still pay the standard bride-price for a virgin.
22.18: You shall not let a sorceress live.
22.19: Whoever lies with an animal must be put to death.
22.20: Whoever offers sacrifice to any god but the LORD alone must be devoted to destruction.
22.21: Do not wrong or oppress the immigrant among you — remember, you were immigrants in Egypt.
22.22: Do not oppress any widow or orphaned child.
22.23: If you do oppress them and they cry out to me, I will hear — I will hear every cry.
22.24: My anger will blaze — I will put you to the sword, and your wives will become the very widows you refused to protect, your children the orphans you would not pity.
22.25: When you lend money to one of my people who is poor, do not play the moneylender — charge no interest.
22.26: If you ever take a man's cloak as a pledge, return it before sundown —
