22.27: for that cloak may be all he has to sleep in. When he cries out to me, I will hear — because I am a God of compassion.
22.28: Do not speak evil of God, and do not curse the ruler of your people.
22.29: Do not hold back your grain and wine offerings. Give me the firstborn of your sons.
22.30: Do the same with your cattle and sheep — they stay with their mothers seven days; on the eighth day they belong to me.
22.31: You are a people set apart for me — holy. Do not eat flesh torn by predators in the field; throw it to the dogs.

23.1: Do not pass along false rumors. Do not side with the guilty by becoming a corrupt witness.
23.2: Do not go along with the crowd when it moves toward evil. When you testify in court, do not twist justice by deferring to popular opinion.
23.3: Do not show favoritism to the poor in a legal dispute either.
23.4: If you find your enemy's ox or donkey wandering, bring it back to him.
23.5: If the donkey of a man who hates you has collapsed under its load, do not walk past — stop and help him lift it.
23.6: Do not twist justice when a poor man brings his case.
23.7: Stay far from false charges. Do not have a hand in putting an innocent or righteous person to death — I will not acquit the wicked.
23.8: Take no bribe. Bribes blind the clear-eyed and corrupt the speech of the honest.
23.9: Do not oppress the immigrant among you. You know what it is to be a stranger — you were strangers in Egypt.
23.10: Six years you sow your land and gather its produce,
23.11: but in the seventh year let the land rest — leave it unworked. The poor may take what grows on its own; what they leave, the wild animals may eat. Do the same with your vineyards and olive groves.
23.12: Work for six days. On the seventh, rest — so your ox and donkey can rest, and the household slave and the immigrant among you can draw breath.
23.13: Observe everything I have commanded you. Do not even speak the names of other gods — let them not be heard from your lips.
23.14: Three times a year you shall hold a festival for me.
23.15: Observe the Feast of Unleavened Bread — seven days of unleavened bread, as I commanded, at the appointed time in the month of Abib, when you came out of Egypt. When you appear before me, come with an offering.
23.16: Celebrate the Feast of Harvest when you bring the first yield of your fieldwork. Celebrate the Feast of Ingathering at year's end, when you have brought in everything from your fields.
23.17: Three times a year every male among you shall present himself before the LORD.
23.18: Do not offer the blood of my sacrifice alongside leavened bread. Do not let the fat from my festival remain until morning.
23.19: Bring the very best of your firstfruits to the house of the LORD your God. Do not boil a kid in its mother's milk.
23.20: Watch: I am sending my angel ahead of you to guard you on the journey and bring you to the place I have prepared.
23.21: Listen to him carefully and do what he says. Do not defy him — he will not overlook your rebellion, for my own authority is within him.
23.22: But if you truly listen to him and do everything I say, I myself will be the enemy of your enemies, the adversary of those who stand against you.
23.23: My angel will lead you into the land of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hivites, and Jebusites — and I will destroy them.
23.24: Do not bow to their gods, do not serve them, do not imitate their practices. Tear their altars down and shatter their sacred pillars.
23.25: Serve the LORD your God — he will bless your food and water and drive sickness far from your midst.
23.26: No woman among you will miscarry or remain barren. I will fill out the full measure of your days.
23.27: I will send my dread ahead of you, throwing every nation you approach into confusion. I will make your enemies turn their backs and flee.
23.28: I will send a swarm of hornets ahead of you to drive out the Hivites, Canaanites, and Hittites before you.
23.29: I will not clear them out in a single year — the land would become wilderness and wild animals would multiply against you.
23.30: Gradually I will drive them out before you, as your numbers grow and you are able to settle the land.
23.31: I will set your territory from the Red Sea to the sea of the Philistines, and from the desert to the Euphrates. I will deliver the people of the land into your hands, and you will drive them out before you.
23.32: You shall make no treaty with them and no alliance with their gods.
23.33: Do not let them settle among you — they will draw you into sin against me. If you worship their gods, that worship will become a trap that catches you.

24.1: Then the LORD said to Moses, 'Come up to me — you and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of Israel's elders. You will worship at a distance.'
24.2: 'Moses alone approaches the LORD. The others must not come near, and the people must not ascend the mountain with him.'
24.3: Moses went back and told the people everything the LORD had said — all the words and all the rulings. The whole assembly answered as one: 'We will do everything the LORD has said.'
24.4: Moses wrote down all the LORD's words. Early the next morning he built an altar at the foot of the mountain and erected twelve pillars — one for each of the twelve tribes of Israel.
24.5: He sent young Israelite men to offer whole burnt offerings and to sacrifice bulls as fellowship offerings to the LORD.
24.6: Moses collected half the blood in bowls and dashed the other half against the altar.
24.7: Then he took the Book of the Covenant and read it aloud to all the people. They said, 'We will do everything the LORD has said, and we will obey.'
24.8: Moses took the blood and sprinkled it on the people, declaring: 'Behold — the blood of the covenant. The LORD has sealed his bond with you in all these words.'
24.9: Then Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders of Israel climbed the mountain.
24.10: and they saw the God of Israel. Under his feet was what seemed like a floor of hammered sapphire — brilliant as the sky itself at noon.
24.11: God did not strike the leaders of Israel. They gazed on God — and then they ate and drank.
24.12: The LORD said to Moses, 'Come up to me on the mountain and wait there. I will give you the stone tablets with the law and commands I have inscribed — for teaching the people.'
24.13: Moses set out with his attendant Joshua, and Moses ascended the mountain of God.
24.14: Moses told the elders, 'Wait here for us until we return. Aaron and Hur are with you — if anyone has a dispute to settle, bring it to them.'
24.15: Moses went up, and cloud enveloped the mountain.
24.16: The glory of the LORD rested on Mount Sinai. The cloud covered it for six days — then on the seventh day the LORD called Moses from within the cloud.
24.17: To the eyes of Israel watching below, the glory of the LORD blazed like devouring fire on the mountaintop.
24.18: Moses walked into the cloud and climbed higher on the mountain. He remained on the mountain forty days and forty nights.

37.1: Bezaleel built the ark from acacia wood: two and a half cubits long, a cubit and a half wide, a cubit and a half tall.
37.2: He sheathed it in pure gold inside and outside, then ran a gold molding around the top.
37.3: He cast four gold rings for the four corners, two on each side.
37.4: He shaped the carrying poles from acacia wood and overlaid them with gold.
37.5: He threaded the poles through the rings along the ark's sides so it could be lifted and carried.
37.6: He fashioned the mercy seat from pure gold: two and a half cubits long, a cubit and a half wide.
37.7: He shaped two cherubim from hammered gold, one at each end of the mercy seat.
37.8: One cherub stood at one end, one at the other — both formed as a single piece with the mercy seat.
37.9: The cherubim spread their wings upward, sheltering the mercy seat beneath them — their faces turned toward one another, inclined downward toward the mercy seat.
37.10: He built the table from acacia wood: two cubits long, one cubit wide, a cubit and a half high.
37.11: He covered it with pure gold and set a gold molding around the top.
37.12: He added a frame a handbreadth wide around the table's edge, with a gold molding running along the top of the frame.
37.13: He cast four gold rings and fixed them at the four leg-corners of the table.
37.14: The rings sat just inside the rim, positioned to receive the carrying poles.
37.15: He made the poles from acacia wood and overlaid them with gold.
37.16: He fashioned all the table's serving vessels from pure gold — plates, incense dishes, bowls, and pitchers for the drink offerings.
37.17: He hammered the lampstand from a single piece of pure gold: base, shaft, almond-shaped cups, calyx buds, and open blossoms — all one.
37.18: Six branches spread from the central shaft — three on each side.
37.19: Every branch bore three almond-shaped cups — each with a calyx bud and an open blossom — on all six branches.
37.20: The shaft itself bore four almond-shaped cups with buds and blossoms.
37.21: Below each pair of branches was a calyx bud — one bud per pair, three pairs in all.
37.22: Buds, branches, every detail — hammered from one undivided piece of pure gold.
37.23: He fashioned seven lamps for it, along with its wick trimmers and catch-trays — all pure gold.
37.24: The lampstand and all its accessories required a full talent of pure gold.
37.25: He made the incense altar from acacia wood: one cubit on each side, a perfect square, two cubits tall, with horns carved from the same piece.
37.26: He overlaid every surface with pure gold — top, sides, and horns — and set a gold molding around the top.
37.27: He fastened two gold rings beneath the molding, one on each side, to receive the carrying poles.
37.28: He made the poles from acacia wood and overlaid them with gold.
37.29: He also compounded the sacred anointing oil and the pure aromatic incense — the craft of a master perfumer.

38.1: He built the burnt offering altar from acacia wood: five cubits on each side — a perfect square — and three cubits high.
38.2: He fashioned horns at all four corners from the same piece, then overlaid everything with bronze.
38.3: Every implement for the altar was fashioned from bronze: pots, shovels, tossing-bowls, flesh-hooks, and firepans.
38.4: He made a bronze lattice grating for the altar, set beneath the surrounding ledge and reaching halfway up.
38.5: He cast four rings at the four corners of the grating to receive the carrying poles.
38.6: He made the poles from acacia wood and overlaid them with bronze.
38.7: He threaded the poles through the rings on both sides so the altar could be carried; the altar itself was built hollow of planks.
38.8: He cast the bronze basin and its stand from the polished metal mirrors surrendered by the women who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting — a luxury offered for sacred use.
38.9: He constructed the court: the south side had curtains of fine twisted linen running a hundred cubits.
38.10: Twenty posts on twenty bronze bases supported them; the hooks and connecting bands were silver.
38.11: The north side matched: a hundred cubits of curtains on twenty posts in twenty bronze bases, silver hooks and bands.
38.12: The west side had fifty cubits of curtains on ten posts and ten bases, with silver hooks and bands.
38.13: The east face was fifty cubits across.
38.14: On one side of the entrance, fifteen cubits of curtains hung on three posts with three bases.
38.15: The same framed the other side of the entrance — fifteen cubits of curtains, three posts, three bases.
38.16: Every curtain enclosing the court was woven from fine twisted linen.
38.17: The bases were bronze, but every hook, connecting band, and post-cap was silver — the entire court ringed with silver-topped pillars.
38.18: The entrance screen was embroidered in blue, purple, and scarlet on fine twisted linen — twenty cubits wide and five cubits tall, matching the surrounding curtains in height.
38.19: Four posts on four bronze bases held it in place, with silver hooks and silver-overlaid tops.
38.20: Every tent peg used throughout the tabernacle and its court was bronze.
38.21: What follows is the official inventory of the tabernacle — the tabernacle of the covenant — compiled at Moses' command, with Ithamar son of Aaron the priest overseeing the Levitical accounting.
38.22: Bezaleel son of Uri son of Hur — of the tribe of Judah — built every piece exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.
38.23: His partner was Oholiab son of Ahisamach of Dan — engraver, master craftsman, and embroiderer in blue, purple, and scarlet linen.
38.24: The total gold expended in the sanctuary's construction — brought as a wave offering — came to twenty-nine talents and seven hundred thirty shekels by the sanctuary weight.
38.25: The silver tallied from the community census came to one hundred talents and seventeen hundred seventy-five shekels by the sanctuary weight.
38.26: Half a shekel per person — one beka apiece — levied on every man twenty years or older counted in the census: 603,550 men in all.
38.27: The hundred talents went entirely to casting the hundred bases — the sanctuary's structural footings and the veil's posts — one talent per base.
38.28: The remaining seventeen hundred seventy-five shekels provided the hooks, the silver caps, and the bands for all the court posts.
38.29: The bronze brought as an offering totaled seventy talents and twenty-four hundred shekels.
38.30: From this he cast the threshold-bases of the tent of meeting, the bronze altar and its lattice grating, and all the altar's implements,
38.31: the bases for the entire surrounding court and its gate, and every peg used throughout the tabernacle and its court.

39.1: From the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn they wove the vestments for holy service and the sacred garments for Aaron — all exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.2: He crafted the ephod from gold thread woven with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn on fine twisted linen.
39.3: They hammered the gold flat, cut it into wire-thin threads, and wove them through the blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and the fine linen — a craftsman's precision art.
39.4: Two shoulder pieces were attached, joining the front and back panels at the top edges.
39.5: The ephod's integrated sash was of the same weave and same materials — gold, blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen — exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.6: They set the onyx stones in gold filigree mounts and engraved on them the names of Israel's twelve tribes — cut as cleanly as a seal.
39.7: He mounted them on the ephod's shoulder pieces — memorial stones for the twelve tribes of Israel — exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.8: He wove the breastplate in the same master-craftsman style as the ephod — gold thread through blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twisted linen.
39.9: Folded double to make a perfect square — a span in each direction.
39.10: Into the breastplate they set four rows of gemstones. First row: carnelian, topaz, emerald.
39.11: Second row: turquoise, lapis lazuli, moonstone.
39.13: Fourth row: beryl, onyx, jasper — each gem held in a gold filigree mount.
39.14: Twelve stones, one for each tribe of Israel — every stone engraved with a tribe's name, cut with the precision of a seal-maker.
39.15: They braided chains of pure gold for the breastplate.
39.16: They made two gold mounts and two gold rings and fastened the rings to the breastplate's upper corners.
39.17: The two braided chains were threaded through the rings at the breastplate's upper corners.
39.18: The chains' other ends they secured in the gold mounts on the ephod's shoulder pieces, in front.
39.19: Two more rings they fixed at the lower corners of the breastplate, on the inside edge that rested against the ephod.
39.20: They fastened two more rings to the lower front of the ephod's shoulder pieces — just above where the sash began.
39.21: Using a blue cord, they lashed the breastplate's lower rings to the ephod's rings, anchoring it above the waistband so it could not come free — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.
39.22: He wove the robe of the ephod entirely in blue — a single seamless piece.
39.23: The neck opening was woven into the center of the garment, its edges bound like a warrior's tunic so the fabric would not split under use.
39.24: Around the robe's hem they attached pomegranates woven in blue, purple, and scarlet on fine twisted linen.
39.25: Between the pomegranates they hung pure gold bells, alternating all the way around the hem.
39.26: Bell, pomegranate, bell, pomegranate — alternating all the way around the hem for sacred service. Exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.27: They wove fine linen tunics for Aaron and his sons,
39.28: the fine linen turban, the fine linen headbands, the fine linen undergarments,
39.29: and the embroidered sash of fine twisted linen worked through with blue, purple, and scarlet — all as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.30: They made the sacred gold medallion and engraved on it — sharp and permanent as a seal — the words: HOLINESS TO THE LORD.
39.31: A blue cord was threaded through it for tying it to the front of the turban — exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
39.32: Every part of the tabernacle — the tent of meeting — was now finished. The Israelites had carried out every command the LORD had given Moses. They had done it all.
39.33: They brought the completed tabernacle to Moses — the tent itself and every accessory: clasps, frames, crossbars, pillars, bases,
39.34: the red-dyed ram-skin covering, the outer fine-leather covering, and the inner veil,
39.35: the ark of the covenant with its poles and the mercy seat,
39.36: the table with its vessels and the arranged bread of the Presence,
39.37: the golden lampstand with its seven lamps set in order, its accessories, and the lamp oil,
39.38: the golden incense altar, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the entrance screen,
39.39: the bronze altar with its lattice grating, poles, and implements, the bronze basin and its stand,
39.40: the court curtains with their pillars and bases, the gate screen with its ropes and pegs — all the equipment for the tent of meeting,
39.41: the vestments for holy service, the sacred garments of Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons for their priestly ministry.
39.42: The Israelites executed every part of the work precisely as the LORD had commanded Moses.
39.43: Moses reviewed the entire construction. When he saw that every piece had been made exactly as the LORD commanded — he blessed them.

40.2: 'On the first day of the new year, erect the tabernacle — the tent of meeting.'
40.3: 'Place the ark of the covenant inside, then draw the inner veil before it.'
40.4: 'Bring in the table and set it in order; bring in the lampstand and light its lamps.'
40.5: 'Place the golden incense altar in front of the ark, and hang the entrance screen.'
40.6: 'Set the burnt offering altar at the tabernacle's entrance.'
40.7: 'Set the bronze basin between the tent of meeting and the altar, and fill it with water.'
40.8: 'Erect the surrounding court and hang the entrance screen.'
40.9: 'Take the anointing oil and anoint the tabernacle — every part of it — and consecrate it with all its furnishings. It will become most holy.'
40.10: 'Anoint the bronze altar and all its implements — consecrate it, and it will be most holy.'
40.11: 'Anoint the basin and its stand and consecrate them.'
40.12: 'Bring Aaron and his sons to the tent of meeting's entrance and wash them with water.'
40.13: 'Clothe Aaron in the sacred vestments, anoint him, and consecrate him — that he may serve before me as priest.'
40.14: 'Bring his sons and put the tunics on them.'
40.15: 'Anoint them just as you anointed their father, that they may serve before me as priests. This anointing will inaugurate an enduring priestly line for all their descendants.'
40.16: Moses carried out every one of these commands. He did exactly as the LORD had instructed.
40.17: On the first day of the first month of the second year — exactly one year from the Exodus — the tabernacle was raised.
40.18: Moses assembled the tabernacle: he laid the bases, stood the frames upright, fitted the crossbars, and raised the pillars.
40.19: He spread the tent covering over the frame, then laid the outer cover on top — as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.20: He placed the covenant tablets inside the ark, fitted the carrying poles, and set the mercy seat over the top.
40.21: He carried the ark into the inner room and drew the veil across, shielding the ark — exactly as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.22: He placed the table on the north side of the tent, outside the inner veil.
40.23: He arranged the bread of the Presence on it before the LORD — as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.24: He set the lampstand on the south side of the tent, directly across from the table.
40.25: He lit the lamps before the LORD — as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.26: He positioned the golden incense altar before the inner veil,
40.27: and burned fragrant incense on it — as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.29: He positioned the burnt offering altar at the tabernacle's entrance and offered the first burnt offering and grain offering on it — precisely as the LORD commanded Moses.
40.30: He set the basin between the tent and the altar and filled it with water for ritual washing,
40.31: and Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's sons washed their hands and feet at it —
40.32: every time they entered the tent of meeting or drew near the altar, they washed. As the LORD commanded Moses.
40.33: He raised the surrounding court around the tabernacle and altar and hung the gate screen. And with that, Moses finished the work.
40.34: Then the cloud descended and covered the tent of meeting — and the glory of the LORD flooded the tabernacle.
40.35: Moses could not enter the tent of meeting — the cloud had come to rest upon it and the radiant glory of the LORD filled it from within.
40.36: Whenever the cloud lifted from the tabernacle, Israel broke camp and moved out,
40.37: but when the cloud did not lift, they stayed put — until it moved.
40.38: The LORD's cloud rested over the tabernacle all day; fire burned within it through every night — a sign visible to every Israelite throughout every stage of the journey.

31.2: 'Look — I have personally chosen Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah. His very name means "in God's shelter," and I have placed him there.'
31.3: 'I have filled him with the Spirit of God — practical wisdom, creative intelligence, deep knowledge, mastery in every form of skilled work.'
31.4: 'He can envision and execute: gold, silver, bronze —'
31.5: 'the cutting and setting of gemstones, the carving of wood, every form of skilled craft.'
31.6: 'And alongside him I have appointed Oholiab son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan — his name means "my father's tent," and he will help build mine. I have placed wisdom in the hearts of every skilled craftsman so that all I have commanded can be made:'
31.7: 'the Tent of Meeting, the ark of the covenant law and its atonement cover, all the furnishings of the Tent —'
31.8: 'the table and all its vessels, the pure gold lampstand with all its fittings, the altar of incense,'
31.9: 'the altar of burnt offering with all its equipment, the washbasin and its base,'
31.10: 'the woven vestments — the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and for his sons in their priestly ministry,'
31.11: 'and the anointing oil and the aromatic incense for the Holy Place. Let them make everything exactly as I have commanded you.'
31.12: The LORD spoke to Moses again:
31.13: 'Tell the people of Israel: My Sabbaths you shall keep above all else — this is the sign between me and you across every generation, the covenant marker by which you will know that I, the LORD, am the one who sets you apart.'
31.14: 'Keep the Sabbath — it is holy, set apart for me and for you. Whoever treats it as ordinary will be put to death; whoever works on it will be cut off from the community of Israel.'
31.15: 'Six days you work — the seventh is Sabbath, complete rest, consecrated to the LORD. Whoever labors on that day must be put to death.'
31.16: 'The people of Israel shall observe the Sabbath — keeping it across every generation as a perpetual covenant bond between us.'
31.17: 'It is an eternal sign between me and Israel: in six days the LORD made sky and earth, and on the seventh day he rested — he let himself be restored. The Sabbath re-enacts creation's completion.'
31.18: When God had finished speaking with Moses on Sinai, he placed in his hands the two stone tablets of the covenant — written by the very finger of God.

32.1: Moses was a long time on the mountain. The people grew restless, assembled around Aaron, and demanded: 'Make us gods to lead us. This Moses who brought us out of Egypt — we have no idea what has become of him.'
32.2: Aaron said to them, 'Pull the gold earrings off the ears of your wives and children and bring them here.'
32.3: The whole assembly pulled off their gold earrings and laid them before Aaron.
32.4: Aaron took the gold, worked it with a tool, and cast it into the shape of a calf. And the people said, 'These are your gods, O Israel — the gods who brought you up from Egypt!'
32.5: Seeing what the people had done, Aaron built an altar before the calf and announced: 'Tomorrow will be a feast day for the LORD.' He tried to contain the catastrophe by wrapping it in the LORD's name.
32.6: The next morning they rose early, offered burnt offerings, and ate fellowship meals. Then the celebration slid into something else — they ate, drank, and gave themselves over to the kind of play that has nothing holy in it.
32.7: The LORD told Moses, 'Go down — now. The people you brought out of Egypt have destroyed themselves.'
32.8: 'They have bolted from the path I set them on — they made a golden calf, bowed to it, slaughtered offerings before it, and declared it the god who rescued them from Egypt. The ink is barely dry on my covenant.'
32.9: 'I have seen this people,' the LORD said to Moses, 'and they are utterly stiff-necked — they bend to no one.'
32.10: 'Now stand aside — let my anger have its way with them. I will consume them and make a great nation from you alone.'
32.11: Moses planted himself before the LORD his God and argued: 'LORD — why would your anger burn against your own people? These are the ones you brought out of Egypt with a display of great power and a strong hand.'
32.12: 'If you destroy them, Egypt will say you brought them into the wilderness to slaughter them — that your rescue was a trap. Turn from your burning anger. Relent from this disaster you have threatened against your own people.'
32.13: 'Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel — your own servants. You swore by yourself, because there was nothing greater to swear by: "I will multiply your descendants like the stars of the sky, and I will give them all this land I promised, as their inheritance forever." Will you now break that oath?'
32.14: And the LORD relented. He did not bring on his people the disaster he had threatened.
32.15: Moses turned and came down the mountain, the two covenant tablets in his hands — inscribed on both sides, front and back.
32.16: The tablets themselves were the work of God; the script carved into them was the writing of God.
32.17: Joshua, waiting lower on the mountain, heard the shouting from the camp and said to Moses, 'It sounds like war down there.'
32.18: Moses said, 'No — not a victory shout, not a battle cry. What I hear is singing.'
32.19: Moses came within sight of the camp — he saw the calf, he saw the dancing — and his anger blazed. He hurled the stone tablets from his hands and shattered them at the base of the mountain.
32.20: He seized the golden calf, melted it down, ground the remains to powder, scattered it over water, and made Israel drink it — forcing them to ingest the proof of their own guilt.
32.21: Moses turned on Aaron: 'What did they do to you that you led them into sin this great?'
32.22: 'Please — don't be furious,' Aaron said. 'You know this people; they are bent toward trouble.'
32.23: 'They said to me, "Make us gods to lead us — Moses who brought us out of Egypt has vanished." What was I supposed to do?'
32.24: 'I told them to give me their gold. They did — I threw it in the fire — and this calf walked out.' Aaron offered the oldest excuse: I am not responsible for what the materials produced.
32.25: Moses saw plainly: the people had broken loose — Aaron had dropped the reins — and they had become a mockery before their enemies.
32.26: Moses stood at the camp gate and called out: 'Whoever is on the LORD's side — come to me.' Every Levite moved to stand with him.
32.27: He told them, 'The LORD God of Israel commands: strap on your swords. Move through this camp from end to end — strike down brother, companion, neighbor.' Covenant loyalty to God takes precedence over every human bond.
32.28: The Levites obeyed. That day approximately three thousand men of the people fell.
32.29: Moses said to the Levites, 'Today you have consecrated yourselves to the LORD's service — you did not spare son or brother. Because of this, he bestows his blessing on you today.' Their willingness to bear cost purchased their vocation.
32.30: The next morning Moses told the people, 'You have committed a catastrophic sin. Now I am going up to the LORD — perhaps I can make atonement for what you have done.'
32.31: Moses returned to the LORD and said, 'This people has committed a terrible sin — they made themselves gods of gold.'
32.32: 'If you will forgive their sin — do so. But if not, then erase me from the book you have written. I will not be saved while they are lost.' Moses offered his own eternal standing as surety for Israel.
32.33: The LORD replied to Moses: 'Each person who has sinned against me bears their own account. I will blot out the guilty — I do not transfer guilt.'
32.34: 'Now go — lead the people toward the land I promised. My angel will go ahead of you. But know this: on the day of my reckoning, I will hold this sin against them.'
32.35: The LORD struck the people with a plague — because of the calf. Aaron had made it, but the people had worshiped it.

33.1: The LORD said to Moses, 'Move out from here — you and the people you brought from Egypt. Head to the land I swore to give to the offspring of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.'
33.2: 'I will send my angel ahead of you to clear the way — Canaanites, Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites, all of them.'
33.3: 'The land is rich — milk and honey. But I will not go up among you. You are stiff-necked, and if I traveled in your midst I might destroy you on the road.'
33.4: When the people heard this devastating news — that God would not go with them — they mourned. No one put on their jewelry.
33.5: The LORD had told Moses, 'Say to Israel: You are stiff-necked. One moment of my presence traveling among you and I would consume you. Strip off your ornaments — I must consider what to do with you.'
33.6: From Mount Horeb onward, Israel put away their jewelry. It was an act of national mourning.
33.7: Moses would take a tent and pitch it well outside the camp, calling it the Tent of Meeting. Anyone who wanted to seek the LORD went there — outside the camp, which had made itself unclean.
33.8: Every time Moses went out to the Tent, the whole camp rose to its feet and stood watching at their own tent doors, eyes following him until he disappeared inside.
33.9: As Moses entered, the pillar of cloud would descend and station itself at the entrance of the Tent. Then the LORD would speak with Moses.
33.10: When all the people saw the pillar of cloud at the Tent entrance, the whole camp rose and worshiped — each from the door of their own tent.
33.11: The LORD spoke to Moses face to face — the way a man speaks openly with his closest friend. Afterward Moses returned to the camp, but his young attendant Joshua son of Nun did not leave the Tent.
33.12: Moses pressed the LORD: 'You keep telling me to lead these people, but you haven't told me who you'll send with me. You said you know me by name — that I've found favor with you.'
33.13: 'If that favor is real, then show me your ways — let me truly know you, not just serve you. And consider this: these people are yours. You cannot abandon your own.'
33.14: The LORD answered simply: 'My presence goes with you. I will give you rest.'
33.15: Moses replied, 'If your presence does not go with us, do not move us from this place. We go nowhere without you.'
33.16: 'How else will the nations know that you are pleased with me and with your people? It is only your presence going with us that sets Israel apart from every other people on earth. Without that, we are nothing special.'
33.17: The LORD said to Moses, 'I will do exactly what you have asked — because you have found favor with me, and because I know you — personally, by name.'
33.18: Moses pushed further: 'Show me your glory.'
33.19: 'I will make all my goodness pass before you, and I will proclaim my own name — the LORD — in your hearing. I will be gracious to whom I choose to be gracious, and I will show compassion on whom I choose to show compassion.' God's mercy is not mechanical; it flows from his sovereign character.
33.20: 'But my face you cannot see. No human being can see me and survive.'
33.21: Then the LORD said, 'There is a place alongside me where you may stand — a rock.'
33.22: 'While my glory passes by, I will shelter you in a cleft of the rock and cover you with my hand until I have gone past.'
33.23: 'Then I will remove my hand, and you will see the afterglow of where I have been — my back. My face remains unseen.'

34.1: The LORD told Moses, 'Cut two stone tablets like the first ones — the ones you shattered. I will write on them again the same words that were on the first tablets.'
34.2: 'Be ready at first light. Come up Sinai in the morning and present yourself to me at the summit.'
34.3: 'Come alone. No one else may be visible anywhere on the mountain — not even flocks or herds grazing near its base.'
34.4: Moses cut two stone tablets like the first ones. He rose early in the morning, climbed Mount Sinai as the LORD had commanded, and carried the two stone tablets in his hands.
34.5: The LORD descended in the cloud and stood there with Moses. He proclaimed his own name: the LORD.
34.6: The LORD passed before him and proclaimed his own character: 'The LORD, the LORD — compassionate and gracious, unhurried in anger, overflowing with covenant loyalty and absolute faithfulness,'
34.7: 'bearing covenant loyalty for thousands of generations, forgiving iniquity and rebellion and sin — yet who will by no means let the guilty go unpunished, carrying the consequences of a father's guilt into the third and fourth generation.' This is the creedal heart of the Hebrew Bible — grace and holiness held together without compromise.
34.8: Moses dropped to the ground and worshiped.
34.9: He prayed, 'Lord — if I have truly found favor with you — go among us, even though we are stiff-necked. Forgive our iniquity and our sin, and claim us as your own inheritance. Do not abandon what belongs to you.'
34.10: The LORD declared, 'I am making a covenant. Before all your people I will perform wonders — things that have never been done in any nation anywhere on earth. All the peoples surrounding you will see the awesome work of the LORD that I am doing with you.'
34.11: 'Keep carefully what I command you today. I will drive out before you the Amorites, Canaanites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.'
34.12: 'Be on your guard. Do not make any treaty with the people of the land you are entering — that path leads to a trap.'
34.13: 'Tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, chop down their Asherah poles.'
34.14: 'Worship no other god. The LORD's name is Jealous — he is a God who does not share what belongs to him.'
34.15: 'Do not make treaties with the people of the land. When they offer sacrifices to their gods and invite you to eat, you will go — and the first step down that road ends in spiritual prostitution.'
34.16: 'When your sons marry their daughters, those daughters will draw your sons after their gods. Generation by generation, the infection spreads.'
34.18: 'Keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread — seven days of unleavened bread at the appointed time in the month of Abib, the month you marched out of Egypt. Let the bread itself remember the exodus.'
34.19: 'Every firstborn belongs to me — the first to open every womb among your livestock, cattle and sheep alike.'
34.20: 'The firstborn donkey — redeem it with a lamb, or break its neck. All your firstborn sons must be redeemed. When you come before me, come with something in your hands.'
34.21: 'Work six days; rest the seventh — even in plowing season, even in harvest. The Sabbath bends for no emergency.'
34.22: 'Keep the Feast of Weeks — the firstfruits of the wheat harvest — and the Feast of Ingathering at year's end.'
34.23: 'Three times every year all your men shall appear before the LORD, the God of Israel.'
34.24: 'I will dispossess the nations before you and enlarge your territory. And when you go up to appear before me three times a year, I will keep your land safe — no one will covet it while you are away.'
34.25: 'Do not offer my sacrificial blood alongside anything leavened. Do not let the Passover sacrifice remain through the night.'
34.26: 'Bring the finest of your firstfruits to the house of the LORD your God. Do not boil a kid in its mother's milk — do not use the source of life to destroy life.'
34.27: The LORD told Moses, 'Write down these words — for by these words I have sealed my covenant with you and with Israel.'
34.28: Moses remained on the mountain with the LORD forty days and forty nights, eating nothing, drinking nothing. He wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant — the Ten Words, the Decalogue.
34.29: Moses came down from Sinai carrying the two covenant tablets. He did not know that the skin of his face had become radiant from his conversation with God.
34.30: Aaron and all Israel looked at Moses — his face blazed with light. They were afraid to approach him.
34.31: Moses called to them. Aaron and all the community leaders came back to him, and Moses spoke with them.
34.32: Then all of Israel came near. Moses gave them every command the LORD had spoken with him on Sinai.
34.33: When Moses finished speaking, he covered his face with a veil.
34.34: But whenever Moses went in before the LORD to speak with him, he removed the veil — and left it off until he came out. Coming out, he told the people what he had been commanded.
34.35: The people could see Moses' face blazing with light. Then Moses would replace the veil until the next time he went in to speak with God.

35.1: Moses gathered the whole community of Israel and said, 'These are the things the LORD has commanded you to do:'
35.2: 'Six days you may work, but the seventh day shall be holy to you — a Sabbath of complete rest for the LORD. Whoever does any work on it must be put to death.'
35.3: 'On the Sabbath day, light no fire in any of your homes.' Even the work of building the tabernacle must yield to the Sabbath.
35.4: Moses addressed the whole assembly of Israel: 'This is what the LORD has commanded:'
35.5: 'From what you have, bring an offering to the LORD. Let everyone whose heart is willing bring gold, silver, and bronze;'
35.6: 'blue, purple, and scarlet thread, fine linen, goats' hair;'
35.7: 'rams' skins dyed red, fine leather, acacia wood;'
35.8: 'oil for the lampstand, spices for the anointing oil and the aromatic incense;'
35.9: 'onyx stones and other gemstones to be set in the ephod and the breastpiece.'
35.10: 'Let every craftsman with skill come and make all that the LORD has commanded:'
35.11: 'the tabernacle — its tent and outer covering, its clasps, frames, crossbars, posts, and bases;'
35.12: 'the ark with its poles, the atonement cover, the inner veil that shields it;'
35.13: 'the table with its poles and all its vessels, the bread of the Presence;'
35.14: 'the lampstand for the light with its fittings, its lamps, and oil for the light;'
35.15: 'the incense altar and its poles, the anointing oil and the aromatic incense, the curtain for the tabernacle entrance;'
35.16: 'the altar of burnt offering with its bronze grate, poles, and all equipment, the bronze washbasin and its base;'
35.17: 'the courtyard hangings with their posts and bases, the curtain for the courtyard gate;'
35.18: 'the tent pegs for the tabernacle and courtyard with their cords;'
35.19: 'the woven vestments for serving in the sanctuary — the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons in their priestly service.'
35.20: The whole community of Israel withdrew from before Moses.
35.21: Then they came — everyone whose heart was stirred, everyone whose spirit moved them — and brought offerings to the LORD for the Tent of Meeting, for all its service, and for the sacred garments.
35.22: Men and women together — all with willing hearts — brought brooches, earrings, signet rings, armbands, every kind of gold ornament. They lifted their gold as an offering to the LORD.
35.23: Everyone who had blue, purple, or scarlet yarn, fine linen, goats' hair, red-dyed rams' skins, or fine leather brought them.
35.24: Those with silver or bronze brought their contributions. Those with acacia wood brought it if it could be used in the work.
35.25: Every woman with skill in spinning worked with her hands and brought what she had made — blue, purple, scarlet yarn, and fine linen.
35.26: And all the women whose hearts were stirred and whose hands were skilled spun the goats' hair.
35.27: The leaders contributed the onyx stones and gemstones for the ephod and breastpiece.
35.28: They also brought spices and oil — for the lampstand, for the anointing oil, and for the aromatic incense.
35.29: Every Israelite man and woman whose heart moved them freely brought it — everything given was a gift of an open hand, a freewill offering to the LORD for all the work Moses had commanded.
35.30: Moses told the Israelites, 'Look — the LORD has called by name Bezalel son of Uri son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.'
35.31: 'He has filled him with the Spirit of God — wisdom, understanding, knowledge, mastery in every craft —'
35.32: 'to design and execute work in gold, silver, and bronze,'
35.33: 'to cut and set stones, to carve wood — every skilled craft.'
35.34: 'And he has given both Bezalel and Oholiab son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan the gift of teaching — not only to make but to transmit the skill.'
35.35: 'He has filled them with every skill needed — engraving, designing, embroidering in blue, purple, and scarlet thread and fine linen, weaving — every kind of skilled work and artistic design.'

36.1: Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman in whom the LORD had placed skill and understanding — everyone capable of doing the sanctuary work — were to carry it out exactly as the LORD had commanded.
36.2: Moses summoned Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman the LORD had gifted — everyone whose heart moved them to come forward and do the work.
36.3: They received from Moses all the contributions the Israelites had brought. And still the people kept bringing freewill offerings morning after morning.
36.4: The craftsmen working on the sanctuary had to stop what they were doing
36.5: and came to Moses with an extraordinary report: 'The people are bringing far more than we need for all this work.'
36.6: Moses issued an order, and it was proclaimed throughout the camp: 'No man or woman is to bring any more for the sanctuary offering.' The people were stopped from bringing.
36.7: What they had already given was more than sufficient for all the work — and then some.
36.8: The skilled craftsmen made the tabernacle itself with ten curtains of fine twisted linen — blue, purple, and scarlet — with cherubim worked into them by expert hands.
36.9: Each curtain measured twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide — all eleven were identical.
36.10: He joined five curtains together into one set and the remaining five into another set.
36.11: Along the outer edge of the last curtain in each set he made loops of blue thread, facing each other.
36.12: Fifty loops on one side, fifty loops on the other — each loop aligned with its pair across the joining.
36.13: He made fifty gold clasps and fastened the two sets of curtains together with them. The tabernacle became one unified whole.
36.14: He made an outer covering of eleven goats' hair curtains to go over the tabernacle.
36.15: Each curtain was thirty cubits long and four cubits wide — all eleven identical.
36.16: He joined five curtains as one set and six as another.
36.17: Fifty loops on the outer edge of each set's connecting curtain, aligned to meet each other.
36.18: Fifty bronze clasps joined the tent into one whole.
36.19: Over the tent he made an outer covering of red-dyed rams' skins and over that a layer of fine leather.
36.20: He made the upright frames for the tabernacle from acacia wood.
36.21: Each frame stood ten cubits high and a cubit and a half wide.
36.22: Each frame had two tenons for locking into bases — identical in construction, every one.
36.23: Twenty frames for the south side of the tabernacle,
36.24: with forty silver bases beneath them — two bases under each frame, one for each tenon.
36.25: Twenty frames for the north side,
36.26: and forty silver bases — two under each frame.
36.27: For the rear wall — the western end — six frames.
36.28: And two frames for the two rear corners.
36.29: The corner frames were joined at the bottom but met at the top in a single ring — both corners built the same way.
36.30: Eight frames in all, sixteen silver bases — two under each frame.
36.31: Five crossbars of acacia wood for the frames along one side of the tabernacle,
36.32: five for the other side, and five for the west end.
36.33: He made the center bar to run the full length of the frames at mid-height, from one end to the other.
36.34: He covered the frames with gold, made gold rings to hold the crossbars, and covered the crossbars with gold.
36.35: He made the inner veil — blue, purple, and scarlet thread with fine twisted linen — cherubim woven through it by expert hands.
36.36: For the veil he made four acacia posts covered with gold, with gold hooks, set in four silver bases.
36.37: For the tent entrance he made a hanging curtain — blue, purple, and scarlet thread with fine twisted linen — the intricate work of an embroiderer.
36.38: Five posts with hooks, their capitals and connecting rods covered with gold — but the five bases were bronze.

25.2: 'Tell the people of Israel to bring me a contribution. Receive it from every person whose heart moves them freely — this is my offering.'
25.3: 'Here is what you shall receive: gold, silver, and bronze;'
25.4: 'blue, purple, and scarlet yarn; fine woven linen; goat hair;'
25.5: 'ram skins dyed red; fine leather; acacia wood;'
25.6: 'olive oil for the lamps; spices for the sacred anointing oil and for the fragrant incense;'
25.7: 'onyx stones and other precious stones to be set in the ephod and in the breastpiece.'
25.8: 'Let them make me a holy place — a sanctuary — so that I may live among them.'
25.9: 'Build it precisely according to everything I am about to show you — the design of the dwelling and the design of all its furnishings, exactly so.'
25.10: 'They shall construct a chest of acacia wood — two and a half cubits long, one and a half wide, one and a half high. This is the ark, the place where my covenant words will rest.'
25.11: 'Overlay it entirely with pure gold — inside and out — and run a gold molding around its top edge.'
25.12: 'Cast four rings of gold and attach them to its four lower corners — two on each side — to receive the carrying poles.'
25.13: 'Make carrying poles of acacia wood, overlaid with gold.'
25.14: 'Slide the poles through the rings on either side of the ark — they are how it will be carried.'
25.15: 'The poles must stay in the rings at all times — they are never to be removed from the ark.'
25.16: 'Into the ark you shall place the testimony — the tablets of the covenant — that I will give you.'
25.17: 'Fashion a cover of atonement from pure gold — two and a half cubits long, one and a half wide. This is the kapporet, the seat of my presence.'
25.18: 'Hammer two cherubim from gold and attach them to the two ends of the cover — one cherub at each end.'
25.19: 'One cherub at this end, one cherub at that end — hammered from the same piece of gold as the cover, a single seamless whole.'
25.20: 'The cherubim spread their wings upward, arching over and sheltering the cover. Their faces turn toward each other, both gazing down toward the cover of atonement — the throne of mercy.'
25.21: 'Set the cover of atonement on top of the ark, and place the covenant tablets inside it.'
25.22: 'There I will meet with you. From above the cover, from between the two cherubim that stand over the ark of the testimony, I will speak to you — giving you all that I command for the people of Israel. The ark is my address; the cover is where heaven and earth touch.'
25.23: 'Make a table of acacia wood — two cubits long, one cubit wide, one and a half cubits high. On it the bread of the Presence will be kept before me always.'
25.24: 'Overlay it with pure gold and run a gold molding around the top.'
25.25: 'Add a frame one handbreadth wide around the top edge, and put a gold molding along that frame.'
25.26: 'Cast four gold rings and fasten them at the four corners where the legs are.'
25.27: 'The rings should sit beside the frame — they receive the carrying poles.'
25.28: 'Make the carrying poles of acacia wood, overlay them with gold — they will bear the table.'
25.29: 'Fashion its plates and dishes, its pitchers and bowls for the drink offerings — all of pure gold.'
25.30: 'Keep the bread of the Presence on this table before me at all times — twelve loaves, one for each tribe, renewed each Sabbath. This is the table of fellowship with the Living God.'
25.31: 'Hammer a lampstand from a single piece of pure gold — base, shaft, cups shaped like almond blossoms, knobs, and petals, all of one hammered piece.'
25.32: 'Six branches extend from its sides — three from each side, like a tree whose light reaches in every direction.'
25.33: 'Each branch bears three almond-blossom cups, each with bud and petal — the same design repeated on all six branches. The lampstand blooms like the almond, the first tree to wake from winter, a sign of watchful life.'
25.34: 'The central shaft bears four almond-blossom cups with buds and petals.'
25.35: 'A single knob at the base of each pair of branches supports them — one knob for each pair, all three knobs integral to the shaft.'
25.36: 'Everything — knobs, branches, cups, shaft — hammered from a single talent of pure gold, seamless. No joint, no weld; one unbroken piece.'
25.37: 'Light its seven lamps so they cast their light forward, illuminating the space before the lampstand. Seven — the number of completeness — for the light that never goes out.'
25.38: 'Its snuffers and trays — all of pure gold.'
25.39: 'One full talent of pure gold for the lampstand and all its accessories — a king's weight of gold for the light that represents God's presence in the holy place.'
25.40: 'Make everything exactly according to the design you were shown on the mountain. Not your invention — God's.'

26.1: Construct the tabernacle with ten curtains — fine woven linen worked in blue, purple, and scarlet — with cherubim figures skillfully woven throughout. The heavenly guardians adorn the place where God dwells.
26.2: Each curtain: twenty-eight cubits long, four wide — every one the same, uniformity in worship.
26.3: Join five curtains into one panel; join the other five into a second panel.
26.4: Sew blue loops along the outer edge of each end panel — corresponding loops that will align when the two panels are joined.
26.5: Fifty loops on each outer edge — fifty on the first panel's end, fifty on the second — aligned so they clasp together perfectly.
26.6: Fifty gold clasps to join the two panels into one — the tabernacle becomes a single, seamless whole. Gold for the innermost layer, because this touches the holy of holies.
26.7: Over the inner linen curtains lay an outer covering of eleven goat-hair panels — the tent that shelters the dwelling.
26.8: Each panel: thirty cubits long, four wide — all eleven the same.
26.9: Join five panels into one set, six into another. Fold the sixth panel double at the entrance — this creates the extra length needed to cover the front.
26.10: Fifty loops on the outer edge of each joined set, corresponding with each other.
26.11: Fifty bronze clasps — bronze for the outer layer, as befits what faces the world — to join the tent panels into one.
26.12: The remaining half panel hangs down over the back of the dwelling — nothing wasted, everything purposeful.
26.13: One cubit of overhang on each side covers and protects the dwelling from the elements — the tent wraps it completely.
26.14: Over the goat-hair tent, a waterproof covering of ram skins dyed red, and over that a protective layer of fine leather. Three layers — the holy wrapped in the ordinary.
26.15: Construct upright frames of acacia wood to form the walls of the dwelling.
26.16: Each frame: ten cubits tall, one and a half wide.
26.17: Each frame has two tenons for fitting into the silver sockets — make every frame the same way.
26.18: Twenty frames for the south wall,
26.19: with forty silver bases beneath them — two bases per frame, one socket for each tenon. The structure rests on silver — the ransom metal of the covenant.
26.20: Twenty frames for the north wall
26.21: with forty silver bases, two per frame.
26.22: Six frames for the rear wall, the western end.
26.23: Two additional corner frames for the rear corners.
26.24: These corner frames are joined from base to top into a single ring — braced for strength, double thickness where the walls meet.
26.25: Eight frames total for the rear wall, sixteen silver bases — two per frame.
26.26: Make five horizontal crossbars of acacia wood for each side wall
26.27: and five for the other side wall, and five for the rear — fifteen bars in all to hold the structure rigid.
26.28: The central crossbar runs the full length of each wall through the middle of the frames — the spine of the dwelling.
26.29: Overlay the frames with gold; make gold rings to hold the bars; overlay the bars themselves with gold — even the structure is pure gold beneath the curtains.
26.30: Erect the tabernacle exactly as it was shown to you on the mountain — divine architecture, not human invention.
26.31: Weave a veil of blue, purple, and scarlet — fine linen with cherubim woven in by a master craftsman. This veil divides the habitable from the inviolable, the holy from the most holy.
26.32: Hang it on four acacia posts overlaid with gold, with gold hooks, set in four silver bases — the veil suspended between heaven's claim and human access.
26.33: Hang the veil below the clasps and bring the ark of the testimony through it into the inner chamber. The veil divides the holy place from the most holy place — the graduated approach to the Holy One.
26.34: Set the cover of atonement on the ark of the testimony in the most holy place — the innermost point of the entire universe, where God's presence rests.
26.35: Outside the veil: the table on the north, the lampstand on the south, facing each other — bread and light, sustenance and illumination, in the holy place.
26.36: For the tent entrance, weave a screen of blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen — needle-worked, beautiful, welcoming and yet marking the threshold of the sacred.
26.37: Five acacia posts overlaid with gold, gold hooks, bronze bases — the entrance frame. Bronze for the world-facing threshold, gold for what belongs to God.

27.1: Build an altar of acacia wood — five cubits square and three cubits high. At this altar, the gifts of the people rise to God in smoke.
27.2: Fashion a horn at each corner, projecting upward, of one piece with the altar — these are where the blood of atonement is applied. Overlay the whole with bronze.
27.3: Ash buckets, shovels, sprinkling bowls, flesh forks, fire pans — every utensil of bronze. Every tool of worship has its proper form.
27.4: Make a bronze grating — a lattice of metalwork — and attach four bronze rings at its four corners.
27.5: Set the grating halfway up the altar, just below the rim — so the coals have air and the fire burns fully.
27.6: Make carrying poles of acacia wood, overlaid with bronze.
27.7: The poles slide through the rings on either side — the altar travels with Israel; worship is not bound to one geography.
27.8: Make it hollow — a wooden frame. Build it exactly as the vision showed you on the mountain.
27.9: Now make a courtyard around the tabernacle. The south wall: one hundred cubits of fine linen curtaining —
27.10: twenty bronze-socketed posts, silver hooks and bands. Bronze at the base where it meets the earth; silver above, gleaming in the sun.
27.11: The north wall mirrors the south: one hundred cubits, twenty posts, bronze bases, silver hooks and bands.
27.12: The western end: fifty cubits of curtain, ten posts, ten bases.
27.13: The eastern end — the entrance side, facing the sunrise — is also fifty cubits wide.
27.14: Fifteen cubits of curtaining on one side of the gate — three posts, three bases —
27.15: and fifteen cubits on the other side — three posts, three bases. Twenty cubits remain for the gate itself.
27.16: The gateway screen is twenty cubits wide — woven in blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen by a skilled embroiderer. Four posts, four bases. Even the entrance to the courtyard is beautiful, because those who draw near to God come through a place worthy of God.
27.17: Every post around the entire courtyard: silver bands and silver hooks, bronze bases — uniform, consistent, no diminishing of honor as you move away from the center.
27.18: The courtyard in full: one hundred cubits long, fifty wide, five cubits high — fine linen walls enclosing the sacred space. Bronze foundations.
27.19: Every peg, every stake, every tool of service for the tabernacle and courtyard — bronze. The practical equipment of worship is not beneath God's specifications.
27.20: Command the people of Israel to bring you pure pressed olive oil for the lamp — so the light in the holy place burns without ceasing. The lamp is never to go out; God's presence is always illuminated.
27.21: In the tent of meeting, outside the veil before the ark of the testimony, Aaron and his sons maintain the light — from evening to morning, before the LORD. A perpetual statute for all generations of Israel. The priests are custodians of the light; they keep watch through the night so God's presence is never without its witness.

28.1: Bring Aaron your brother and his sons — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar — out from among the Israelites to serve me as priests. From the whole nation, these are set apart.
28.2: Make sacred garments for Aaron your brother — garments that speak of dignity and beauty. The priest who stands before God must be clothed in a way that honors God.
28.3: Speak to every craftsman whose heart I have filled with skill — commission them to make Aaron's garments for his ordination, so he may minister as my priest. The gift of skill is from God; it is put to its highest use in service to God.
28.4: The garments to be made: a breastpiece, an ephod, a robe, an embroidered tunic, a turban, and a sash. Make these sacred garments for Aaron and his sons, so they may minister as my priests.
28.5: The materials: gold thread, blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and fine linen — the same elements that mark God's dwelling throughout.
28.6: Craft the ephod from gold thread and blue, purple, and scarlet yarn woven into fine linen — the highest craft for the highest garment.
28.7: Two shoulder pieces join the front and back at the top, fastening the ephod securely to Aaron's shoulders.
28.8: The waistband of the ephod is woven from the same materials — gold, blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen — and is one piece with the ephod, not a separate addition.
28.9: Take two onyx stones and engrave on them the names of the twelve sons of Israel —
28.10: six names on one stone, six on the other, in birth order. Israel's sons are named on the high priest's shoulders: he carries the nation before God.
28.11: Engrave the stones like a master gem cutter engraves a seal — permanent, precise — and set them in gold filigree work.
28.12: Set the two stones on the shoulder pieces of the ephod — memorial stones for the sons of Israel. When Aaron stands before the LORD, he carries the nation's name on his shoulders. The priest is the bearer of his people.
28.14: and two braided gold chains — twisted like rope — attached to the filigree settings. These will connect the shoulder stones to the breastpiece.
28.15: Make the sacred breastpiece — the breastpiece of judgment, for discerning God's will — crafted with the same skill and the same materials as the ephod: gold, blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen.
28.16: It shall be square and folded double — one span each way — forming a pouch.
28.17: Set four rows of stones in it. First row: carnelian, chrysolite, emerald —
28.18: second row: turquoise, lapis lazuli, moonstone —
28.20: fourth row: chrysolite, onyx, jasper — all set in gold. Twelve stones for twelve tribes.
28.21: Twelve stones, one for each tribe — each engraved with a name like a royal seal. Israel's identity is literally written on the heart of the high priest.
28.22: Attach twisted gold chains — braided like rope — at the top corners of the breastpiece.
28.23: Two gold rings at the upper corners of the breastpiece —
28.24: thread the two braided gold chains through these rings,
28.25: and attach the other ends to the filigree settings on the ephod's shoulder pieces — at the front. The breastpiece hangs from the ephod; the names of the tribes hang over Aaron's heart.
28.26: Make two more gold rings for the lower inner corners of the breastpiece — the corners that face the ephod.
28.27: And two more gold rings on the lower front of the ephod's shoulder pieces, just above the waistband — these anchor the breastpiece at the bottom.
28.28: Tie the breastpiece's lower rings to the ephod's rings with blue cord, keeping the breastpiece snug above the waistband — so it never slips loose. The sacred garments form a unified whole.
28.29: Every time Aaron enters the holy place, he carries Israel's twelve names over his heart. He is their representative before God — bearing them into the divine presence that ordinary Israelites cannot enter. A memorial, a pleading, a perpetual act of intercession.
28.30: Place the Urim and Thummim inside the breastpiece — the sacred lots by which God's will is discerned. They rest over Aaron's heart when he stands before the LORD. He does not guess; he does not speculate: he inquires of God, and God answers. The high priest carries Israel's judgment into the very presence of the Judge.
28.31: Make the robe worn under the ephod entirely of blue — the color of heaven, worn next to the priest's body.
28.32: The neck opening must have a woven reinforced edge — like the collar of a battle garment — so the robe does not tear. The high priest's garments are never to be damaged. Aaron's predecessor-desecration is the warning: garments torn in the holy place call down death.
28.33: Around the hem of the robe: pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet alternating with gold bells — pomegranate, bell, pomegranate, bell, all the way around.
28.34: The pomegranates represent fruitfulness and the fullness of the law; the bells announce the priest's movement. Everyone outside must know when Aaron is moving through the holy place, lest anyone enter unexpectedly into the zone of holiness and die.
28.35: Aaron wears this robe whenever he ministers. The sound of the bells is heard as he enters the holy place before the LORD and as he leaves — so that he will not die. The sound signals his continued life; silence would mean catastrophe.
28.36: Engrave a medallion of pure gold with the words: HOLY TO THE LORD — cut deep as a royal seal. This is the crown of the high priest's identity: not his own name, but a declaration of whose he is.
28.37: Attach it to the turban with a blue cord, worn on the front of the turban — the first thing anyone sees when the high priest faces them.
28.38: It rests on Aaron's forehead — and through it, Aaron absorbs whatever fault or impurity taints the sacred offerings of Israel. Their gifts come before God imperfect; the high priest bears the discrepancy on his forehead. It is always there so that Israel's offerings are always accepted. The priest takes on what would otherwise disqualify.
28.39: Weave the tunic of fine linen; fashion the turban of fine linen; embroider the sash — each piece given the craft it deserves.
28.40: For Aaron's sons: tunics, sashes, and caps — made for dignity and beauty. Even the lesser priests wear garments worthy of their calling.
28.41: Dress Aaron and his sons, anoint them, fill their hands with offerings, and sanctify them — so they may serve as my priests. Clothing alone does not make the priest; the anointing sets them apart.
28.42: Make linen undergarments covering from the waist to the thigh — modesty before God is not optional.
28.43: Aaron and his sons must wear them whenever they enter the tent of meeting or approach the altar — so they do not incur guilt and die. A permanent statute for Aaron and all his descendants: the holy place requires full priestly dress. To enter improperly is to die.

29.1: This is the ordination ceremony — how you will set them apart to minister as my priests: bring a young bull and two rams, each without defect.
29.2: From the finest wheat flour make unleavened bread: plain loaves, loaves mixed with oil, and thin wafers brushed with oil — no leaven, for this is consecration, not ordinary food.
29.3: Place all the bread in a basket and bring it, along with the bull and the two rams, to the tent entrance.
29.4: Bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance of the tent of meeting and wash them with water. Ordination begins with cleansing — the priest approaches God washed.
29.5: Dress Aaron: first the tunic, then the robe of the ephod, then the ephod itself, and the breastpiece — and fasten the ephod's waistband around him. Layer by layer, he becomes the high priest.
29.6: Place the turban on his head and affix the sacred medallion — HOLY TO THE LORD — to the front of the turban. The last thing put on is the first thing seen.
29.7: Pour the sacred anointing oil on his head — the anointing makes him the Anointed One, the Meshiach, the one set apart by God's own authority.
29.8: Now bring Aaron's sons and dress them in their tunics
29.9: and tie sashes on them and set caps on their heads. The priesthood belongs to them by perpetual statute — it is their inheritance from God, not an achievement. Consecrate Aaron and his sons.
29.10: Bring the bull before the tent of meeting. Aaron and his sons lay their hands on its head — the gesture of identification, of transferring guilt. The animal stands in for the people.
29.11: Slaughter the bull before the LORD at the tent entrance. Life given so that others may draw near.
29.12: With your finger, apply blood to the horns of the altar — the four points of contact between sacrifice and God. Pour the remaining blood at the base of the altar. The altar is purged, made ready for holy use.
29.13: Take the fat that covers the entrails, the liver's lobe, and the two kidneys with their surrounding fat — and burn it all on the altar. These are the richest parts, the best given to God.
29.14: The flesh, hide, and offal of the bull are burned outside the camp — because this is a purification offering. What bears the impurity does not come to the altar; it is removed far from the holy place.
29.15: Now take the first ram. Aaron and his sons lay their hands on its head.
29.16: Slaughter it; take the blood and dash it against the altar on all sides.
29.17: Cut the ram in pieces, wash the entrails and legs, and reassemble all the pieces with the head.
29.18: Burn the whole ram on the altar — a whole burnt offering to the LORD, a pleasing aroma, a fire-offering that ascends entirely to God. Nothing held back.
29.19: Take the second ram — the ram of ordination. Aaron and his sons lay their hands on its head.
29.20: Slaughter it. Apply blood to the right earlobe of Aaron and each of his sons — the organ of hearing, to consecrate what they hear. Then to the right thumb — to consecrate what they do with their hands. Then to the right big toe — to consecrate where they walk. Sprinkle the rest on the altar. The priest is consecrated from ear to toe; every faculty set apart.
29.21: Sprinkle blood from the altar mixed with anointing oil on Aaron and his garments, on his sons and their garments. The garments become holy along with the men who wear them. Priest and vestments are inseparable in the eyes of God.
29.22: From the ordination ram take: the fat, the fat tail, the covering fat of the entrails, the liver's lobe, the two kidneys with their fat, and the right thigh — the prime cut, the best, for this is the ram of consecration.
29.23: From the basket before the LORD: one plain loaf, one oiled loaf, one thin wafer — the bread that belongs to God.
29.24: Place all of it — the fat portions and the bread — in the hands of Aaron and his sons. Let them wave it before the LORD — this is the 'filling of the hands,' the act that defines ordination. The priest's hands are literally filled with his first offering.
29.25: Take the portions from their hands and burn them on the altar with the burnt offering — a pleasing aroma rising before the LORD. The first priestly act is completed.
29.26: Take the breast of Aaron's ordination ram and wave it before the LORD — this breast becomes your own portion, Moses. The one who ordains the priest receives a share.
29.27: Set apart the waved breast and the presented thigh of the ordination ram as sacred — belonging to Aaron and his sons.
29.28: This becomes a permanent statute: the breast and the thigh belong to Aaron and his sons forever, contributed by Israel from their fellowship offerings. The priest is sustained by the gifts of the people he mediates for.
29.29: Aaron's sacred garments pass to his sons after him — they are worn at each subsequent ordination. The office outlasts the man; the garments are the continuity.
29.30: Whichever son succeeds Aaron as high priest wears the garments for seven full days upon entering the tent of meeting — the same seven-day pattern of consecration, repeated for every generation.
29.31: Boil the ordination ram's meat in a sacred location.
29.32: At the tent's entrance, Aaron and his sons eat the ram's flesh and the bread from the basket. Ordination ends with a sacred meal — priest and God's provision consumed together.
29.33: They eat the very offerings through which their atonement was made and their ordination completed. No outsider may eat — these things are holy, consumed only by those who have been made holy through them.
29.34: If any meat or bread remains until morning, burn it — it must not be eaten. That which belongs to God's holy day does not carry over. Each day's worship is complete in itself.
29.35: Carry out every part of this ceremony for Aaron and his sons, exactly as I have commanded, for seven full days. Ordination is not rushed. Seven days — the fullness of a week, the number of completion.
29.36: Each day of the seven, offer a bull as a purification offering. Purge the altar through the atonement rite, and anoint it to consecrate it. The altar itself must be made holy before it can make others holy.
29.37: Seven days of atonement for the altar; at the end it is most holy — so holy that whatever touches it is drawn into its holiness. The altar becomes a holy magnet.
29.38: This is the perpetual offering — the tamid — that will be offered on the altar every single day: two one-year-old lambs.
29.39: One lamb at dawn. One lamb at dusk. Morning and evening, the day is bracketed by worship.
29.40: With the morning lamb: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of pressed olive oil, and a quarter hin of wine as a libation.
29.41: The evening lamb with the same grain offering and libation as the morning — a pleasing aroma, a fire-offering ascending to the LORD. Day begins and ends in worship.
29.42: This perpetual burnt offering continues through all your generations — offered at the entrance of the tent of meeting before the LORD. It is the standing appointment: the place where God meets his people, day after day, without fail.
29.43: There I will meet with all of Israel — and the dwelling will be made holy by my glory. God's presence sanctifies the place where he chooses to be.
29.44: I will consecrate the tent of meeting. I will consecrate the altar. I will consecrate Aaron and his sons. God himself does the consecrating — human ceremony is the vehicle, but divine action is the reality.
29.45: And I will dwell among the people of Israel and be their God. This is the purpose of everything — the ark, the curtains, the priests, the offerings: God coming home to live among his people.
29.46: They will know it: I am the LORD their God — the one who brought them out of Egypt for this very purpose, that I might dwell in the midst of them. The Exodus was not only liberation from slavery. It was the beginning of the journey home to God. I am the LORD their God.

30.1: Build an altar of acacia wood for burning incense. This altar stands before the veil — the fragrance of prayer ascending before the throne.
30.2: One cubit square, two cubits tall — twice the height of its width, proportioned like a pillar. Its horns of one piece with it.
30.3: Overlay its top, sides, and horns entirely with pure gold, and add a gold molding around the top. Gold throughout — this altar touches the holiest space.
30.4: Two gold rings below the molding, one on each side, for the carrying poles.
30.5: Carrying poles of acacia wood overlaid with gold.
30.6: Place it before the veil that hangs before the ark of the testimony — before the cover of atonement. This is where I will meet with you. The incense altar marks the approach to the divine address.
30.7: Every morning when Aaron tends the lamps, he burns fragrant incense on this altar. Prayer accompanies the lighting of the light — they are one act of approach.
30.8: And at twilight when he lights the lamps again, he burns incense again. Morning and evening — the perpetual incense before the LORD, rising without ceasing through all your generations.
30.9: On this altar: only the sacred incense. No other incense — nothing unauthorized. No burnt offering, no grain offering, no libation. This altar has one purpose: to carry the fragrance of prayer before God.
30.10: Once a year — on the Day of Atonement — Aaron applies blood to its horns, making atonement for it. The incense altar too must be purged of accumulated impurity. Once a year, through all generations. It is most holy to the LORD.
30.12: 'When you take a census of Israel and number the people, each person counted must give the LORD a ransom for his life. To be counted is to be known by God — and every life before God requires atonement. Without this, the census itself brings death.'
30.13: 'Every person who is numbered gives half a shekel — by the sanctuary standard, where twenty gerahs make a shekel. A half shekel: the same for each person. This is the atonement levy for the LORD.'
30.14: 'Everyone twenty years and older who is counted must give this offering to the LORD. No exemptions — adulthood brings accountability.'
30.15: 'The rich give no more; the poor give no less: half a shekel each. Before God, atonement is equal. Wealth buys no extra standing; poverty does not diminish it. One price for all lives.'
30.16: 'Collect this atonement money from Israel and direct it to the service of the tent of meeting. It becomes a memorial before the LORD — Israel's collective ransom, remembered before God. The tent of God's presence is sustained by the ransom of God's people.'
30.18: 'Make a bronze basin on a bronze stand and place it between the tent of meeting and the altar. Fill it with water. Between sacrifice and the holy place stands the water of cleansing.'
30.19: 'Aaron and his sons wash their hands and feet from it — hands for what they do, feet for where they go.'
30.20: 'Every time they enter the tent of meeting, they wash — so that they will not die. Every time they approach the altar to offer a fire-offering to the LORD, they wash. The penalty for unwashed approach is death. Holiness requires clean hands.'
30.21: 'They wash hands and feet — so they will not die. A permanent statute for Aaron and all his descendants through every generation. The requirement of cleansing never lapses.'
30.23: 'Take the choicest spices: five hundred shekels of liquid myrrh, two hundred fifty shekels of fragrant cinnamon, two hundred fifty shekels of aromatic calamus —'
30.24: 'five hundred shekels of cassia — measured by the sanctuary shekel — and one hin of olive oil.'
30.25: 'Blend these into a sacred anointing oil — mixed by a skilled perfumer, holy. This is the oil that sets people and things apart for God.'
30.26: 'With this oil anoint the tent of meeting and the ark of the testimony —'
30.27: 'the table and all its articles, the lampstand and its accessories, the incense altar —'
30.28: 'the altar of burnt offering and all its utensils, the basin and its stand.'
30.29: 'Consecrate them all so they become most holy. Whatever touches them is drawn into their holiness. The anointing transfers sacred status.'
30.30: 'Anoint Aaron and his sons and consecrate them — so they may minister to me as priests. The same oil that anoints the furniture anoints the people. Priest and sanctuary share one consecration.'
30.31: 'Tell the Israelites: this oil is the sacred anointing oil before me for all your generations.'
30.32: 'Do not pour it on an ordinary person's body. Do not replicate its formula for personal use. It is holy — reserved entirely for God's purposes. To misuse it is to treat the holy as ordinary.'
30.33: 'Anyone who makes a copy of this oil, or who anoints an unauthorized person with it, shall be cut off from the people. The boundary of the holy is not to be crossed.'
30.34: The LORD said to Moses: 'Take these fragrant spices in equal parts — stacte, onycha, galbanum, and pure frankincense —'
30.35: 'and blend them into a sacred incense — a perfumer's craft, salted, pure, and holy. Nothing improvised; God specifies the fragrance of his house.'
30.36: 'Grind some of it fine and place it before the ark of testimony in the tent of meeting — where I meet with you. It is most holy to you. The finest incense marks the closest point of access to God.'
30.37: 'Do not make this incense formula for your own use. It belongs entirely to the LORD — holy to him alone.'
30.38: 'Anyone who makes this blend to enjoy as personal fragrance shall be cut off from the people. What is holy to God is not to be domesticated for human pleasure.'
